kubernetes/kubernetes#135417.body:
### What happened?

Not entirely sure whether this is desired behavior or not, but `defaulter-gen` would generate `zz_generated.defaults.go`, even when no `// +k8s:defaulter-gen` tag exists.

`deepcopy-gen` for example would not generate anything if there is no tag found.

https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/5bcb7599736327cd8c6d23e398002354a6e40f68/staging/src/k8s.io/code-generator/cmd/deepcopy-gen/generators/deepcopy.go#L148-L160

Same pattern is followed by `validation-gen`:

https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/5bcb7599736327cd8c6d23e398002354a6e40f68/staging/src/k8s.io/code-generator/cmd/validation-gen/targets.go#L305-L312

However, `defaulter-gen` would happily generate a file, no matter whether the tag exists or not.

https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/5bcb7599736327cd8c6d23e398002354a6e40f68/staging/src/k8s.io/code-generator/cmd/defaulter-gen/generators/defaulter.go#L333-L336

### What did you expect to happen?

When there is no `// +k8s:defaulter-gen` tag there should be no generated files.

### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

Create a new Go project.

```
mkdir example-project
cd example-project
go mod init example-project
```

Install `defaulter-gen`.

```
go get -v -tool k8s.io/code-generator/cmd/defaulter-gen
```

Create a placeholder package.

```
mkdir -p pkg/apis/
echo 'package apis' > pkg/apis/apis.go
```

Run `defaulter-gen` against it.

```
go tool defaulter-gen ./pkg/apis/...
```

A defaults file will be generated.

```

$ tree
.
├── go.mod
├── go.sum
└── pkg
    └── apis
        ├── apis.go
        └── generated.defaults.go

3 directories, 4 files
```

### Anything else we need to know?

_No response_

### Kubernetes version

<details>

```console
$ kubectl version
# paste output here
```

</details>

# paste output here

# paste output here

C:\> wmic os get Caption, Version, BuildNumber, OSArchitecture
# paste output here
```

kubernetes/kubernetes#135475.body:
For the beta promotion of `DRADeviceBindingConditions` (DBC), we have listed below the metrics that are planned for implementation to bring this feature to a production-ready state.

1.  #### _The number of times and the status BindingConditions are processed during PreBind_
	Counts the number of scheduling attempts where at least one `ResourceClaim` is allocated to a device with `BindingConditions`. In addition, by specifying `success`, `failure`, or `timeout` with the label `status`, users can view the counts for each BindingConditions processing status. This allows an operator to determine if the feature is in use by workloads.

2. #### _PreBind duration with vs without BindingConditions_
	A latency histogram for the PreBind phase that can toggle between views with and without `BindingConditions`.

These metrics are based on the comments proposed by @johnbelamaric in kubernetes/enhancements#5487.

If there are any other metrics that should be implemented, please propose them in this issue.

- [ ] Code (`k/k`) update PR(s):
kubernetes/kubernetes#135475.title: DRA: DBC - metrics for production readiness

kubernetes/kubernetes#135512.body:
### What happened?

In the `k8s.io/kubectl/pkg/drain` package, the daemonset filter uses `context.TODO()` instead of the drain helper's context. [Source code permalink](https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/61c629cc57649bf0ce2378903027c83939d84109/staging/src/k8s.io/kubectl/pkg/drain/filters.go#L196).

This has implications for time outs and for logging, where any loggers passed as part of the context will be used for all calls except this one.

### What did you expect to happen?

I expected this function to use the context included in the Helper struct if available via the `getContext` method

### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

n/a, although you will notice the logging effect if you pass a `logr.Discard()` to the `Helper`. When running `RunNodeDrain` on a high verbosity, you will see many logs printed from the DaemonSet get call

### Anything else we need to know?

_No response_

### Kubernetes version

<details>

Noticed in `k8s.io/kubectl v0.33.6`, present in master branch still.

kubernetes/kubernetes#135558.body:
### What would you like to be added?

A new alpha command, `kubectl alpha kuberc set` has been added, which modifies the contents of the `kuberc` file. We should modify the interface to `kubectl alpha kuberc set` to make it possible to set the `credentialPluginPolicy` and `credentialPluginAllowlist` fields (added in 1.35 as an extension to KEP-3104) from the command line.

### Why is this needed?

This is needed in order to further reduce the friction for end-users in securing the `kubectl` client. `credentialPluginPolicy` and `credentialPluginAllowlist` are security knobs, and it would be great to have a simple cli one-liner to turn them on, rather than requiring the user to modify and/or create a new configuration file with which they may not be familiar.  In addition, the appropriate values may depend on provider configurations, but cloud providers can likewise provide CLI one-liners to allow their plugins but no others.
kubernetes/kubernetes#135558.title: Make credential plugin policy/allowlist settable with `kubectl kuberc set`

kubernetes/kubernetes#135622.body:
### What happened?

We observed the e2e test `External Storage [Driver: disk.csi.azure.com] [Testpattern: Dynamic PV (default fs)] read-write-once-pod [MinimumKubeletVersion:1.27] should preempt lower priority pods using ReadWriteOncePod volumes` causes flakes in other e2e tests in a downstream CI job with Azure Disk CSI driver.

[The test](https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/4e94e70dcad423e9f59f12ac5a048d2137d20e86/test/e2e/storage/testsuites/readwriteoncepod.go#L133) runs a default (low-prio) pod1 with a `ReadWriteOncePod` volume and then starts a high prio pod2 that tries to use the same volume. To run pod2, the scheduler has to evict pod1 to make the RWOP volume "free" for pod2. That works great, however, it may evict also pods of other e2e tests that run with the default priority.

What happened on a busy clusters, with all nodes at their attachment limit.

1. pod1 runs, pod2 is created.
2. The scheduler evicts pod1. That frees the RWOP volume and it also frees the last attachment slot on the node.
3. Some other e2e tests creates a Pod and scheduler puts it on the node, taking the last attachment slot.
4. The scheduler schedules pod2 agaian and it sees there is no node with a free attachment slot -> new round of eviction, now evicting a pod of unrelated e2e tests. The unrelated test will fail.  

We observed it with Azure, which can attach only 8 disks on the cheaper nodes, so it's more reproducible there.

### What did you expect to happen?

The preemption test should not affect other e2e tests

### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

See above

### Anything else we need to know?

_No response_

### Kubernetes version

<details>

```console
$ kubectl version
# paste output here
```

</details>


### Cloud provider

<details>

</details>

<details>

# On Linux:
$ cat /etc/os-release
# paste output here
$ uname -a
# paste output here

# On Windows:
C:\> wmic os get Caption, Version, BuildNumber, OSArchitecture
# paste output here
```

kubernetes/kubernetes#135628.body:
### What happened?

The SELinux warning controller emits metrics + events when two pods that have different SELinux labels and share the same volume. This may not be supported when SELinuxMount feature gets GA. See the [KEP](https://github.com/kubernetes/enhancements/tree/master/keps/sig-storage/1710-selinux-relabeling).

However, the controller reports these conflicting SELinux labels also for Pods in Failed or Succeeded phase. Kubelet is umounting volumes from these pods by default, so they cannot conflict with any other volume.

### What did you expect to happen?

The controller should ignore Succeeded and Failed pods.

### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

Run a pod that uses a PVC with SELinux label `s0:c0,c1` to completion (either Succeeded or Failed). Run a second pod that uses the same volume and has label `s0:c98,c99`. The controller now emits an event that these pods are conflicting. But the first one is finished and its volumes are unmounted, so they don't really conflict.

### Anything else we need to know?

_No response_

$ kubectl version
# paste output here

kubernetes/kubernetes#135639.body:
### What happened? It seems like there would have more and more users run kubernetes with nftables, and at the latest blog post for 1.35, said `For Linux nodes, the recommended kube-proxy mode is already [nftables](https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/networking/virtual-ips/#proxy-mode-nftables).` https://kubernetes.io/blog/2025/11/26/kubernetes-v1-35-sneak-peek/ However, while running `kube-proxy` with the following `kube-proxy-config` ```yaml apiVersion: kubeproxy.config.k8s.io/v1alpha1 kind: KubeProxyConfiguration .. nftables: minSyncPeriod: 0s # default, tried 1s, 2s, 3s, 4s, 5s, ..., 10s, all lead to the same result. syncPeriod: 30s # default ... mode: "nftables" # explicitly as nftables ... ``` With the cluster scale growing up, it would somehow trigger `watchdog: BUG: soft lockup - CPU#XXX stuck for ###s! [nft:######]` causing noticeable slowness of kube-proxy rule update, the soft lockup time could up to hundreds of seconds. is about `kernel-6.12.55, m6i.8xlarge (32 vCPU, 128 GiB)` ```log I1129 08:26:30.747856 1 proxier.go:1193] "SyncProxyRules complete" ipFamily="IPv4" elapsed="2m13.763323781s" I1129 08:50:20.799683 1 proxier.go:1199] "Syncing nftables rules" ipFamily="IPv4" fullSync=false ``` ```log I1129 09:28:48.774320 1 proxier.go:1193] "SyncProxyRules complete" ipFamily="IPv4" elapsed="2m12.955796557s" I1129 09:28:49.250671 1 proxier.go:1199] "Syncing nftables rules" ipFamily="IPv4" fullSync=false ``` Also tested with `kernel-6.1.144, m6i.8xlarge (32 vCPU, 128 GiB)` ``` I1129 08:24:33.332403 1 proxier.go:1840] "Reloading service nftables data" ipFamily="IPv4" numServices=5006 numEndpoints=250011 I1129 08:37:39.986065 1 proxier.go:1193] "SyncProxyRules complete" ipFamily="IPv4" elapsed="13m15.34210662s" ``` The worst case scenario could up to `elapsed="20m...."` which might be too long for the production environment. --- Looking at top/htop, would seeing it was busy doing `/usr/sbin/nft -f -`, `/usr/sbin/nft --json list chains ip`, etc. --- --- > I didn't capture the `htop` result for `m6i.8xlarge (32 vCPU, 128 GiB)`, the above screenshots was about `m5.4xlarge (16 vCPU, 64 GiB)`. ### What did you expect to happen? `kube-proxy` should not occupy that much CPU times and better have differential ruleset load but not load it at once. Yes, I'm aware it's how `nftables` design which documented at [KEP-3866 - Low level design](https://github.com/kubernetes/enhancements/blob/master/keps/sig-network/3866-nftables-proxy/README.md#design-details) ### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)? Scripted test for reproduce the issue have been documented down at the repository below, * https://github.com/guessi/kube-proxy-benchmark Note, the test was performed with latest version as well. ### Anything else we need to know? To be clear, the scripts in my repository is aim to reproduce the issue quick The real world case don't needs that much rule set to. We have reproduced the issue at 1000+ services scale, the "service" here is referring to `Kind: Service`, it doesn't need to be too large scale. ### Kubernetes version
```console $ kubectl version --client Client Version: v1.34.2 Kustomize Version: v5.7.1 ```

kubernetes/kubernetes#135662.body: ### What happened? The `kubelet_http_requests_duration_seconds` histogram metric always records near-zero values (~2 microseconds) regardless of actual request processing time. Below is a screenshot from a downstream CI run showing the average latency of kubetet's /metrics/cadvisor endpoint: ### What did you expect to happen? The metric should record the actual time taken to handle each HTTP request, from when it's received until the response is sent. ### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)? 1. Make an HTTP request to the kubelet that takes a measurable amount of time (e.g., `/pods/` endpoint) 2. Query the `kubelet_http_requests_duration_seconds` metric 3. Observe that the recorded duration is always ~2 microseconds instead of the actual processing time ### Anything else we need to know? **Root cause:** In `pkg/kubelet/server/server.go`, the defer statement evaluates `SinceInSeconds(startTime)` immediately when the defer is declared, not when it executes: ```go defer servermetrics.HTTPRequestsDuration.WithLabelValues(...).Observe(servermetrics.SinceInSeconds(startTime)) ``` In Go, arguments to deferred function calls are evaluated immediately at the defer statement. This means `SinceInSeconds(startTime)` computes the duration at the start of the request (~5µs after `startTime` was set), rather than at the end when the defer actually runs. This affects observability and SLO tracking for kubelet HTTP endpoints since duration data is meaningless. ### Kubernetes version 1.33
kubernetes/kubernetes#135662.title: kubelet_http_requests_duration_seconds metric records incorrect (near-zero) latency values

kubernetes/kubernetes#135684.body:
### What happened?

Missing request total apiserver latency and components break down in the audit log annotation causes trouble when we want to narrow down which component took longer for a specific slow request. 

In the audit http filter, `writeLatencyToAnnotation` will track latency in annotation only when the total latency of the given request exceeds 500ms. The total latency is calculated by subtracting request start time `ac.GetEventRequestReceivedTimestamp().Time` from end time `ac.GetEventStageTimestamp()`. 

However, the end time is not assigned until the audit log event is persisted to the audit backend [here](https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/612122f1d79eb148b06bb63fbf392c03f9b73d60/staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/endpoints/filters/audit.go#L107)

The issue was probably introduced in https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/129472

### What did you expect to happen?

audit log with latency annotation when the request latency exceeds 500ms

### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

1. remove the 500ms threshold
2. `./hack/local-up-cluster.sh`

```

kubernetes/kubernetes#135713.body:
### Which jobs are flaking?

https://prow.k8s.io/view/gs/kubernetes-ci-logs/logs/ci-kubernetes-e2e-kind-ipv6/1998907708706656256

### Which tests are flaking?

Kubernetes e2e suite.[It] [sig-node] Pods Extended Pod Container Status should never report container start when an init container fails

### Since when has it been flaking?

[2025-12-11 00:08:52 +0000 UTC](https://prow.k8s.io/view/gs/kubernetes-ci-logs/logs/ci-kubernetes-e2e-kind-ipv6/1998907708706656256)

### Testgrid link

https://testgrid.k8s.io/sig-release-master-blocking#kind-ipv6-master

### Reason for failure (if possible)

{ failed [FAILED] 1 errors:
pod pod-terminate-status-0-6 on node kind-worker container unexpected exit code 2: start=2025-12-11 00:21:17 +0000 UTC end=2025-12-11 00:21:17 +0000 UTC reason=Error message=
In [It] at: k8s.io/kubernetes/test/e2e/node/pods.go:1381 @ 12/11/25 00:21:49.467
}

### Anything else we need to know?

When runc's execution of a container is sent a sigterm / sigkill signal before it has handed off to the container process with execve, it exits with exit code 2.

This is actually orthogonal to what this test was *trying* to verify, which is that stopped containers do not report *success*, and do not re-run, and stay failed.

Possible fix for improving runc's mapping of signal-terminated runs to expected error codes in 
https://github.com/opencontainers/runc/pull/5189

Relaxing the e2e test to tolerate exit code 2 as another possible expected non-success code in https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/137749

### Relevant SIG(s)

/sig testing
/sig node
kubernetes/kubernetes#135713.title: [Flaky test] [sig-node] Pods Extended Pod Container Status should never report container start when an init container fails - unexpected exit code 2

kubernetes/kubernetes#135741.body:
## Problem

The `appProtocol` field (GA since v1.20) is not displayed in `kubectl describe service` output, even when set. Users must use `kubectl get svc -o yaml` to verify this field.

This can be problematic when debugging Service Mesh or Gateway API configurations where `appProtocol` affects traffic routing. Given that `appProtocol` has been GA for several years and is actively used in Service Mesh environments, displaying it in `kubectl describe` output would be beneficial for users.

## Proposed Solution

Display `appProtocol` in the port section of `kubectl describe service` output:
```
Port:              http  80/TCP
TargetPort:        8080/TCP
AppProtocol:       http          # <-- Add this
NodePort:          http  30080/TCP
```

https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/master/staging/src/k8s.io/kubectl/pkg/describe/describe.go#L3015

## Benefits

- Consistency with other port fields (`name`, `protocol`, `port`, `targetPort`, `nodePort`)
- Easier debugging for Service Mesh and Gateway API users

kubernetes/kubernetes#135757.body:
### What happened?

The kube-apiserver crashed with a fatal error `concurrent map read and map write` during CEL expression evaluation for ValidatingAdmissionPolicy.

The crash occurs in `k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/cel/types.go:463` inside `FindStructFieldType` when reading from a `DeclType.Fields` map that is being concurrently written to.

**Stack trace (reader goroutine):**
```
fatal error: concurrent map read and map write

goroutine 11296835574 [running]:
internal/runtime/maps.fatal({0x3a59a3a?, 0xc633d97608?})
  runtime/panic.go:1058 +0x18
k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/cel.(*DeclTypeProvider).FindStructFieldType(0xc026d9dce0, {0x3a32069, 0x14}, {0xc07763b2e0, 0xc})
  k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/cel/types.go:463 +0x74
k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/cel.(*DeclTypeProvider).FindStructFieldType(0xc045262180, {0x3a32069, 0x14}, {0xc07763b2e0, 0xc})
  k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/cel/types.go:460 +0x21f
github.com/google/cel-go/interpreter.(*attrFactory).NewQualifier(...)
  github.com/google/cel-go@v0.23.2/interpreter/attributes.go:226 +0x9f
...
k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/admission/plugin/cel.(*CompositedConditionEvaluator).ForInput(...)
  k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/admission/plugin/cel/composition.go:188 +0x189
k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/admission/plugin/policy/validating.(*validator).Validate(...)
  k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/admission/plugin/policy/validating/validator.go:122 +0x772
...
k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/endpoints/handlers.(*patcher).patchResource.func2()
  k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/endpoints/handlers/patch.go:704 +0xa7
```

I haven't managed to capture the writer, but I suspect it is the `AddField` method since it writes to `c.MapType.Fields[name]`

**Root Cause guess:**

I'm guessing there is a race condition in `staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/admission/plugin/cel/composition.go`. The `NewCompositedCompilerFromTemplate` function shares the same `MapType` (and its `Fields` map) across all `CompositedCompiler` instances:

```go
// composition.go:72-77
func NewCompositedCompilerFromTemplate(context *CompositionEnv) *CompositedCompiler {
  context = &CompositionEnv{
    MapType:           context.MapType,  // SHARED - same pointer!
    EnvSet:            context.EnvSet,
    CompiledVariables: map[string]CompilationResult{},
  }
```

The singleton template is created via `getCompositionEnvTemplateWithStrictCost()` in `plugin.go:49-55`.

When multiple goroutines process admission requests concurrently:
- **Reader**: `FindStructFieldType` at `types.go:463` reads from `st.Fields[fieldName]`
- **Writer**: `AddField` at `composition.go:127` writes to `c.MapType.Fields[name]`

### What did you expect to happen?

No fatal errors

### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

Sorry to say I don't have a great answer here. My best guess at the root cause points towards:

1. Create a ValidatingAdmissionPolicy with CEL expressions that use variables
2. Generate high concurrent load to the API server (multiple PATCH/UPDATE requests) that trigger that policy while also updating VAPs (triggering further compilation)

### Anything else we need to know?

The crash was observed in a production environment under normal operational load. The error message preceding the crash was:
```
E1212 20:54:45.514642 12 finisher.go:175] "Unhandled Error" err="FinishRequest: post-timeout activity - time-elapsed: 1.691904645s, panicked: false, err: context canceled, panic-reason: <nil>" logger="UnhandledError"
```


### Kubernetes version

```
$ kubectl version
Client Version: v1.33.5
Server Version: v1.33.5
```

### Cloud provider

AWS EKS

### OS version

not sure (whatever EKS uses, presumably Amazon Linux 2023)

### Install tools

n/a

### Container runtime (CRI) and version (if applicable)

n/a

### Related plugins (CNI, CSI, ...) and versions (if applicable)

n/a

TestReplaceEvents/Delete_all_objects

_No response_

```

_No response_

kubernetes/kubernetes#135835.body:
### What happened?

When a Pod references a `ResourceClaimTemplate` (RCT) that doesn't exist at pod creation time, and the RCT is created later, there can be significant delays before the pod gets scheduled.

**Root Cause:**

The ResourceClaim controller in kube-controller-manager uses `AddRateLimited()` when the referenced ResourceClaimTemplate is not found. This applies dual rate limiting:

1. **Exponential backoff (per-item):** `5ms × 2^failures`, capped at 1000 seconds
2. **Token bucket (global):** 10 QPS with 100-token burst

The final delay is `MAX(exponential_delay, token_bucket_delay)`.

**The Problem:**

When the ResourceClaimTemplate is eventually created, there is no mechanism to:
1. Notify the ResourceClaim controller to immediately reprocess waiting pods
2. Move affected pods from `unschedulablePods` queue back to `activeQ` in the scheduler

The pod must wait for its current backoff period to expire before the controller retries, which can be up to 1000 seconds if the pod has already failed multiple times.

### What did you expect to happen?

When a new `ResourceClaimTemplate` is created, pods that were waiting due to the missing template should be processed promptly (within seconds), not after potentially long backoff delays.

### How to reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

1. Create a Pod that references a non-existent ResourceClaimTemplate:

```yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: test-pod
spec:
  containers:
  - name: test
    image: nginx
    resources:
      claims:
      - name: my-claim
  resourceClaims:
  - name: my-claim
    resourceClaimTemplateName: my-template  # Does not exist yet
```
2. Wait for several retry cycles (observe increasing backoff delays in controller logs)
3. Create the ResourceClaimTemplate:
```yaml
apiVersion: resource.k8s.io/v1
kind: ResourceClaimTemplate
metadata:
  name: my-template
spec:
  spec:
    devices:
      requests:
      - name: device
        deviceClassName: example-class
```
kubernetes/kubernetes#135835.title: Slow pod scheduling when ResourceClaimTemplate is created after pod creation due to exponential backoff

kubernetes/kubernetes#135865.body:
### What happened?

The `ci-kubernetes-unit-dependencies` CI job is failing after updating `go.opentelemetry.io/contrib/instrumentation/google.golang.org/grpc/otelgrpc` from v0.60.0 to v0.64.0.

**Failed job**: https://prow.k8s.io/view/gs/kubernetes-ci-logs/logs/ci-kubernetes-unit-dependencies/2002001097211777024

### Root Cause

Commit [07504e453](https://github.com/open-telemetry/opentelemetry-go-contrib/commit/07504e45307f41b8b4e609324a3b62374ad6008f) in otelgrpc removed nil checks from `WithTracerProvider` and `WithMeterProvider` options. When Kubernetes passes a nil `TracerProvider` in `staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/storage/storagebackend/factory/etcd3.go:326`:

```go
otelgrpc.WithTracerProvider(c.TracerProvider),  // c.TracerProvider can be nil
```

The nil now overwrites the default global provider, causing a panic in `NewClientHandler()` when it calls `c.TracerProvider.Tracer(...)`.

/kind bug
/priority important-soon
kubernetes/kubernetes#135865.title: ci-kubernetes-unit-dependencies failing due to otelgrpc v0.64.0 nil pointer panic

kubernetes/kubernetes#135883.body:
### What happened?

When kube-apiserver is deployed on a new control-plane node before kube-proxy is running, the AvailableConditionController, which checks the availability of registered APIServices, may connect to the Service’s ClusterIP before it is proxied by kube-proxy. As a result, the connection can be routed to an external server that happens to be listening on the same IP, causing the APIService to be reported as unhealthy due to unexpected responses.

However, even after the kube-proxy runs and proxys the ClusterIP, because of transport-layer connection caching and the fact that kube-apiserver is hard-coded to skip server certificate validation (even when the APIService’s `insecureSkipTLSVerify` is set to false), the APIService can remain unhealthy until the kube-apiserver is restarted:
https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/5151f58ef08d9fb26931019d6d844292aeb57637/staging/src/k8s.io/kube-aggregator/pkg/controllers/status/remote/remote_available_controller.go#L179-L184



### What did you expect to happen?

kube-apiserver should be able to recover from corner cases where it mistakenly establishes a connection to an incorrect server that never returns the expected response during APIService availability checks. It can be addressed either by verifying the server’s certificate when the APIService’s caBundle is provided, or by avoiding reuse of cached connections when the server returns an unexpected response.

### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

1. Install an APIService with a caBundle configured and backed by a ClusterIP Service.
2. Run an HTTPS API server outside the cluster that listens to the ClusterIP and is routable from the cluster and returns 404 for unknown paths.
3. Add a new control-plane node where kube-apiserver starts before kube-proxy is scheduled on the node.
4. Check the kube-apiserver logs: the APIService continues to be reported as unhealthy due to 404 status code even after kube-proxy is running on the node.

### Anything else we need to know?

_No response_

### Kubernetes version

<details>

```console
$ kubectl version
# paste output here
```

</details>


### Cloud provider

<details>

</details>

# On Linux:
$ cat /etc/os-release
# paste output here
$ uname -a
# paste output here

# On Windows:
C:\> wmic os get Caption, Version, BuildNumber, OSArchitecture
# paste output here
```

</details>

kubernetes/kubernetes#135901.body:
### What happened?

When using Dynamic Resource Allocation (DRA) with ResourceClaims, kubelet fails with:

    Error: internal error: unable to get claim info for ResourceClaim <name>

The error occurs when:
1. A first pod is created that references a single ResourceClaim.
2. A second pod is created that references the same ResourceClaim plus an additional one.

The failure happens during container creation, after scheduling succeeded.

The issue is reproducible using the dra-example-driver from kubernetes-sigs.

Potential spots where log may occurs:
https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/release-1.34/pkg/kubelet/cm/dra/manager.go#L417
https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/release-1.34/pkg/kubelet/cm/dra/manager.go#L442
https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/release-1.34/pkg/kubelet/cm/dra/manager.go#L498

### What did you expect to happen?

The second pod should successfully start with both ResourceClaims prepared.

Notably, the same set of ResourceClaims works correctly when requested in the opposite order:
- When the first pod uses both ResourceClaims, and the second pod uses only one - Works ✅ 
- When the first pod uses one ResourceClaim, and the second pod then uses both - Not working 🔴 

### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

1. Deploy the dra-example-driver:
   https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/dra-example-driver

2. Create two ResourceClaims:
```yaml
apiVersion: resource.k8s.io/v1
kind: ResourceClaim
metadata:
  name: first-resource-claim
spec:
  devices:
    requests:
    - name: request
      exactly:
        deviceClassName: gpu.example.com
        allocationMode: ExactCount
        count: 1
---
apiVersion: resource.k8s.io/v1
kind: ResourceClaim
metadata:
  name: second-resource-claim
spec:
  devices:
    requests:
    - name: request
      exactly:
        deviceClassName: gpu.example.com
        allocationMode: ExactCount
        count: 1
```

3. Create a first pod that references only the first ResourceClaim:
```yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: test-pod-1
spec:
  containers:
  - name: container
    image: busybox
    command: ["sleep", "infinity"]
    resources:
      claims:
      - name: first
  resourceClaims:
  - name: first
    resourceClaimName: first-resource-claim
  restartPolicy: Never
```

4. Wait until the pod is running.
5. Create a second pod that references both ResourceClaims:
```yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: test-pod-2
spec:
  containers:
  - name: container
    image: busybox
    command: ["sleep", "infinity"]
    resources:
      claims:
      - name: first
      - name: second
  resourceClaims:
  - name: first
    resourceClaimName: first-resource-claim
  - name: second
    resourceClaimName: second-resource-claim
  restartPolicy: Never
```


### Anything else we need to know?

_No response_

### Kubernetes version

<details>

```console
$ kubectl version
Version: v1.34.1+k3s1
```

</details>



### OS version

<details>

```console
# On Linux:
$ cat /etc/os-release
PRETTY_NAME="Ubuntu 24.04.3 LTS"
NAME="Ubuntu"
VERSION_ID="24.04"
VERSION="24.04.3 LTS (Noble Numbat)"
VERSION_CODENAME=noble
ID=ubuntu
ID_LIKE=debian
HOME_URL="https://www.ubuntu.com/"

BUG_REPORT_URL="https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/"
PRIVACY_POLICY_URL="https://www.ubuntu.com/legal/terms-and-policies/privacy-policy"
UBUNTU_CODENAME=noble
LOGO=ubuntu-logo
$ uname -a
Linux 6.11.0-17-generic #17~24.04.2-Ubuntu SMP PREEMPT_DYNAMIC Mon Jan 20 22:48:29 UTC 2 x86_64 x86_64 x86_64 GNU/Linux

kubernetes/kubernetes#135924.body:
### What happened?

The OLM operator crashed with a nil pointer dereference panic in client-go/tools/record/events_cache.go:53 within the getEventKey function. The panic occurred when strings.Join was called with a slice containing nil string values, likely due to `event` being nil.
```go
2025-12-17T02:12:17.881293848Z E1217 02:12:17.881155       1 panic.go:262] "Observed a panic" panic="runtime error: invalid memory address or nil pointer dereference" panicGoValue="\"invalid memory address or nil pointer dereference\"" stacktrace=<
2025-12-17T02:12:17.881293848Z  goroutine 120 [running]:
2025-12-17T02:12:17.881293848Z  k8s.io/apimachinery/pkg/util/runtime.logPanic({0x337f8f8, 0xc005bccde0}, {0x29a5ca0, 0x4d76d40})
2025-12-17T02:12:17.881293848Z          /build/vendor/k8s.io/apimachinery/pkg/util/runtime/runtime.go:132 +0xbc
2025-12-17T02:12:17.881293848Z  k8s.io/apimachinery/pkg/util/runtime.handleCrash({0x337f9d8, 0x4e90e40}, {0x29a5ca0, 0x4d76d40}, {0x4e90e40, 0x0, 0x442fb8?})
2025-12-17T02:12:17.881293848Z          /build/vendor/k8s.io/apimachinery/pkg/util/runtime/runtime.go:107 +0x116
2025-12-17T02:12:17.881293848Z  k8s.io/apimachinery/pkg/util/runtime.HandleCrash({0x0, 0x0, 0xc000bf3500?})
2025-12-17T02:12:17.881293848Z          /build/vendor/k8s.io/apimachinery/pkg/util/runtime/runtime.go:64 +0x105
2025-12-17T02:12:17.881293848Z  panic({0x29a5ca0?, 0x4d76d40?})
2025-12-17T02:12:17.881293848Z          /usr/lib/golang/src/runtime/panic.go:792 +0x132
2025-12-17T02:12:17.881293848Z  strings.(*Builder).WriteString(...)
2025-12-17T02:12:17.881293848Z          /usr/lib/golang/src/strings/builder.go:108
2025-12-17T02:12:17.881293848Z  strings.Join({0xc001862b08?, 0x127e2?, 0xb}, {0x0, 0x0})
2025-12-17T02:12:17.881293848Z          /usr/lib/golang/src/strings/strings.go:510 +0x22a
2025-12-17T02:12:17.881293848Z  k8s.io/client-go/tools/record.getEventKey(0xc001083340?)
2025-12-17T02:12:17.881293848Z          /build/vendor/k8s.io/client-go/tools/record/events_cache.go:53 +0x175
2025-12-17T02:12:17.881293848Z  k8s.io/client-go/tools/record.(*EventAggregator).EventAggregate(0xc0004d7720, 0xc000c78008)
2025-12-17T02:12:17.881293848Z          /build/vendor/k8s.io/client-go/tools/record/events_cache.go:242 +0x7e
2025-12-17T02:12:17.881293848Z  k8s.io/client-go/tools/record.(*EventCorrelator).EventCorrelate(0xc00035c420, 0x10137f901?)
2025-12-17T02:12:17.881293848Z          /build/vendor/k8s.io/client-go/tools/record/events_cache.go:510 +0x29
2025-12-17T02:12:17.881293848Z  k8s.io/client-go/tools/record.(*eventBroadcasterImpl).recordToSink(0xc000bfc300, {0x336e5e0, 0xc000c33710}, 0xc000caea08, 0xc00035c420)
2025-12-17T02:12:17.881293848Z          /build/vendor/k8s.io/client-go/tools/record/event.go:293 +0xc5
2025-12-17T02:12:17.881293848Z  k8s.io/client-go/tools/record.(*eventBroadcasterImpl).StartRecordingToSink.func1(0xc001862fa8?)
2025-12-17T02:12:17.881293848Z          /build/vendor/k8s.io/client-go/tools/record/event.go:279 +0x29
2025-12-17T02:12:17.881293848Z  k8s.io/client-go/tools/record.(*eventBroadcasterImpl).StartEventWatcher.func1()
2025-12-17T02:12:17.881293848Z          /build/vendor/k8s.io/client-go/tools/record/event.go:418 +0x126
2025-12-17T02:12:17.881293848Z  created by k8s.io/client-go/tools/record.(*eventBroadcasterImpl).StartEventWatcher in goroutine 1
2025-12-17T02:12:17.881293848Z          /build/vendor/k8s.io/client-go/tools/record/event.go:404 +0xb2
2025-12-17T02:12:17.881293848Z  >
2025-12-17T02:12:17.884482085Z panic: runtime error: invalid memory address or nil pointer dereference [recovered]
2025-12-17T02:12:17.884482085Z  panic: runtime error: invalid memory address or nil pointer dereference
2025-12-17T02:12:17.884482085Z [signal SIGSEGV: segmentation violation code=0x1 addr=0x0 pc=0x485365]
2025-12-17T02:12:17.884482085Z 
```

### What did you expect to happen?

The getEventKey function should handle nil fields gracefully instead of panicking. It should either:
  1. Check for nil before accessing nested fields and use empty strings as defaults
  2. Return an error that can be handled by the caller
  3. Skip events with incomplete data

### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

  This occurred in an OpenShift environment during operator installation/reconciliation. The logs before the panic show:
  - Multiple "resource name may not be empty" errors from the OperatorCondition controller
  - Conflict errors when updating Operator status
  - Rapid CSV reconciliation for servicemeshoperator3.v3.2.0 and kiali-operator.v2.17.2

  The panic happened in the event broadcaster goroutine, which runs independently from the main reconciliation loops.

### Anything else we need to know?

  The getEventKey function in events_cache.go constructs an event key by joining multiple fields:
```go
  The `getEventKey` function in `events_cache.go` constructs an event key by joining multiple fields:

  func getEventKey(event *v1.Event) string {
      return strings.Join([]string{
          event.Source.Component,      // panics if event is nil
          event.Source.Host,
          // ...
      }, "")
  }
```
  If `event` (which is a pointer `*v1.Event`) is nil, accessing `event.Source` causes
  a nil pointer dereference. The function lacks a nil check for the `event` parameter
  before accessing its fields.

# paste output here
```

kubernetes/kubernetes#135929.body:
### What happened?

1. created a pod A
2. pod A starting `mountDevice` , but at this time, the csi-plugin pod was upgrading, kubelet received `rpc error: code = Unavailable error reading from server: EOF`, marked the volume device  `uncertain`, actually the globalmount was mounted.
https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/15673d04e30c711a7bb0f0efe6abf4baead1463b/pkg/volume/util/operationexecutor/operation_generator.go#L690-L694
https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/15673d04e30c711a7bb0f0efe6abf4baead1463b/pkg/volume/csi/csi_client.go#L449-L451
https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/15673d04e30c711a7bb0f0efe6abf4baead1463b/pkg/volume/csi/csi_client.go#L711-L728
3. kubelet retried to mountDevice for pod A, but at this time. the csi-plugin pod was terminated and had not started again, the kubelet received `rpc error: code = Unavailable desc = connection error: desc = "transport: Error while dialing: dial unix xxx/csi.sock: connect: connection refused`, it was not a `UncertainProgressError` and then marked the volume device  `unmounted`. Actually it failed at `csi.NodeSupportsStageUnstage(ctx)` this time, hadn't called `csi.NodeStageVolume` yet.
https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/15673d04e30c711a7bb0f0efe6abf4baead1463b/pkg/volume/util/operationexecutor/operation_generator.go#L674-L683
https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/15673d04e30c711a7bb0f0efe6abf4baead1463b/pkg/volume/csi/csi_client.go#L704-L706
4. pod A was deleted, but the volume device in asw was unmounted, kubelet didn't call umountDevice, then the globalmount was residual.


I think that while a volume was uncertain, kubelet retried to mount it again, althrough kubelet may received a not `UncertainProgressError`, we should not change it's status to unmounted, it should be always uncertain until called unmountDevice successfully

### What did you expect to happen?

globalmount can be unmounted under any circumstances

### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

above

### Anything else we need to know?

_No response_

kubernetes/kubernetes#135941.body:
### Which jobs are flaking?

`k8s.io/kubernetes/pkg/apiserver/pkg/server/filters` unit tests. Observed in:

- ci-kubernetes-unit-ppc64le
- ci-kubernetes-unit-1-34
- ci-kubernetes-unit-1-32

### Which tests are flaking?

`TestPriorityAndFairnessWithPanicRecoveryAndTimeoutFilter` Specifically sub-tests involving timeout scenarios:

- `priority_level_concurrency_is_set_to_1,_request_times_out_and_inner_handler_hasn't_written_to_the_response_yet`
- `priority_level_concurrency_is_set_to_1,_inner_handler_panics_after_the_request_times_out`

### Since when has it been flaking?

Intermittently observed

### Testgrid link

https://storage.googleapis.com/k8s-triage/index.html?text=TestPriorityAndFairnessWithPanicRecoveryAndTimeoutFilter&test=k8s.io%2Fapiserver%2Fpkg%2Fserver%2Ffilters

### Reason for failure (if possible)

There is an inherent race condition in the test setup between the TimeoutHandler and the APF (Priority and Fairness) tracking logic.

The test expects APF headers (like X-Kubernetes-PF-FlowSchema-UID) to be present on all responses. However, when a request times out (Status 504), the outer TimeoutHandler takes over, writes the response, and closes the stream/context.

If the TimeoutHandler finalizes the response before the inner APF handler has a chance to write its tracking headers, the headers will be missing from the response. The test currently fails an assertion when these headers are missing.

Sample Failure Log:

`priority-and-fairness_test.go:788: Expected APF headers to match, but got: expected HTTP header X-Kubernetes-PF-FlowSchema-UID to have value "test-fs", but got: ""`

### Anything else we need to know?

I have successfully reproduced this flake locally on x86 environments. I have a fix prepared which updates the test helper (`headerMatcher.inspect`) to skip APF header assertions if the request context is already cancelled or timed out.

### Relevant SIG(s)

/sig api-machinery
kubernetes/kubernetes#135941.title: [Flaky test] [REDACTED_TOKEN] flakes on APF header assertions

kubernetes/kubernetes#135953.body:
### What would you like to be added?

fake.Clientset suffers from a race condition related to informers: it does not implement resource version support in its Watch implementation and instead assumes that watches are set up before further resource changes are made.

If a test waits for caches to be synced and then immediately adds an object, that new object will never be seen by event handlers if the race goes wrong and the Watch call hadn't completed yet (can be triggered by adding a sleep before https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/b53b9fb5573323484af9a19cf3f5bfe80760abba/staging/src/k8s.io/client-go/tools/cache/reflector.go#L431).

Example test:

https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/3c2e339be8a7dbed3f3918c8a33edf41726f22f9/pkg/controller/replicaset/replica_set_test.go#L1214-L1234

This is becoming more of a problem in https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/135395 because it makes `informerFactory.WaitForCacheSync` return more quickly, thus increasing the risk that the race goes wrong.

There are two workarounds:

- Explicitly wait for watches:

```Go
	var numWatches atomic.Int32
	clientSet.PrependWatchReactor("*", func(action core.Action) (handled bool, ret watch.Interface, err error) {
		var opts metav1.ListOptions
		if watchActcion, ok := action.(core.WatchActionImpl); ok {
			opts = watchActcion.ListOptions
		}
		gvr := action.GetResource()
		ns := action.GetNamespace()
		watch, err := clientSet.Tracker().Watch(gvr, ns, opts)
		if err != nil {
			return false, nil, err
		}
		numWatches.Add(1)
		return true, watch, nil
	})

       ktesting.Eventually(tCtx, func(tCtx ktesting.TContext) int32 {
		return numWatches.Load()
	}).WithTimeout(5*time.Second).Should(gomega.Equal(int32(5)), "All watches should be registered.")
```

- Convert the test to run in a synctest bubble (`tCtx.SyncTest` when using ktesting, `synctest.Test` in plain Go), then add `tCtx.Wait` or `synctest.Wait` after or instead of  `informerFactory.WaitForCacheSync`.

A better solution would be to make fake client-go behave properly.

/sig api-machinery

### Why is this needed?

Avoid test flakes.
kubernetes/kubernetes#135953.title: fake client-go: support ResourceVersion in Watch

kubernetes/kubernetes#135960.body:
### What would you like to be added?

I propose restructuring the cacher package to improve modularity and reduce test execution time.

The restructuring should be done in two phases:

1. Extract Testing Infrastructure Move shared test helpers into a new package, e.g., pkg/storage/cacher/testing. This package should include: dummy, testSetup and their helpers https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/135961
2. Extract Subpackages Once the testing infrastructure is independent, move the following components into their own subpackages. Initial ideas:
    * consistency https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/135961
    * delegator
    * ready
    * timebudget
    * watchcache
    * store https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/135958 

The main cacher package should focus on Cacher struct and it's related functions 

### Why is this needed?

The cacher package tests are running for over 2.5 minutes, and on multiple cases adding any new test caused it hit to the 3-minute timeout limit for packages ad. This high duration makes the tests flaky and prone to timeouts in CI environments.

The cacher package has become monolithic, containing several distinct components (delegator, 
ready, time_budget, watch_cache) that are tightly coupled. 

Without direct effort to split the package we cannot easily extract these components because of high coupling of components and testing depending on shared utilities. 

EDIT: Also unblock https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/131862

/cc @liggitt @wojtek-t
kubernetes/kubernetes#135960.title: Restructure cacher package to reduce test duration and improve modularity

kubernetes/kubernetes#135968.body:
### What happened?

When a Service has zero backing Pods (e.g., Deployment scaled to 0 or no Pods ever matched the selector), the EndpointSlice object returned by the API server contains:
```
{
  "kind": "EndpointSliceList",
  "apiVersion": "discovery.k8s.io/v1",
  "metadata": {
    "resourceVersion": "828044619"
  },
  "items": [
    {
      "metadata": { ... },
      "addressType": "IPv4",
      "endpoints": null,  // null here
      "ports": null
    }
  ]
}
```
This breaks clients / validators that rely on the published OpenAPI schema where `endpoints` is treated as a required field and modeled as an array (not nullable).

### What did you expect to happen?

According to the API Spec, `endpoints` should be a JSON array. For the “no endpoints” case, it should be an empty array.
```
"endpoints": []
```
Ref.: https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/master/api/openapi-spec/v3/apis__discovery.k8s.io__v1_openapi.json#L228-L231



### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

```
kubectl scale rs/replicaset.apps/demo-5f6c9cb999 --replicas=0
```

### Anything else we need to know?

- The API type defines `Endpoints []Endpoint` without `omitempty`, so it’s expected to be present and modeled as a list in generated schema/docs: https://pkg.go.dev/k8s.io/api/discovery/v1#EndpointSlice
- A related client breakage report (Java client) describing the same behavior: https://github.com/kubernetes-client/java/issues/3226 

### Kubernetes version

AWS EKS v1.32
```
Client Version: v1.34.1
Kustomize Version: v5.7.1
Server Version: v1.32.9-eks-3025e55
Warning: version difference between client (1.34) and server (1.32) exceeds the supported minor version skew of +/-1
```

### Cloud provider

AWS EKS

_No response_

_No response_

### Container runtime (CRI) and version (if applicable)

_No response_

### Related plugins (CNI, CSI, ...) and versions (if applicable)

_No response_
kubernetes/kubernetes#135968.title: EndpointSlice API returns `endpoints: null` even though OpenAPI schema defines `endpoints` as required array

kubernetes/kubernetes#135980.body:
### What happened?

I'm using https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/master/staging/src/k8s.io/kube-aggregator/pkg/client/clientset_generated/clientset/fake/clientset_generated.go in my tests and I now get the following deprecation warning:
```
pkg/server/plugin/notifier/k8sbundle/k8sbundle_test.go:906:12: SA1019: fakeaggregator.NewSimpleClientset is deprecated: NewClientset replaces this with support for field management, which significantly improves server side apply testing. NewClientset is only available when apply configurations are generated (e.g. via --with-applyconfig). (staticcheck)
        client := fakeaggregator.NewSimpleClientset()
```

### What did you expect to happen?

I'd have expected the package to provide a `NewClientset` function, as the deprecation warning specifies. No such functions is available.

### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

Grep for `NewClientset` in https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/master/staging/src/k8s.io/kube-aggregator/pkg/client/clientset_generated/clientset/fake/clientset_generated.go.

### Anything else we need to know?

_No response_

### Kubernetes version

Was not a problem with k8s.io/kube-aggregator v0.34.2 but it's a problem with v0.35.0

kubernetes/kubernetes#136003.body:
### What would you like to be added?

I would like to propose addressing several observability gaps in the WatchList feature (introduced in [KEP-3157](https://github.com/kubernetes/enhancements/tree/master/keps/sig-api-machinery/3157-watch-list)). In Kubernetes 1.35 the controllers transitioned from the standard LIST operation to WatchList for fetching initial object sets, the  feature currently lacks the parity in logging and tracing required for effective production debugging.

**Overview of WatchList:**
WatchList allows controllers to bypass the initial `LIST` request in the List-Watch protocol by initiating a single `WATCH` request that includes the initial set of objects. While this reduces server-side memory spikes and improves scalability, the current streaming nature of the response bypasses existing discrete-step tracing and logging mechanisms.

### **Observability Comparison: LIST vs. WatchList**

| Feature | LIST (Legacy) | WatchList (New) | OSS Gaps to Address |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Initial log** | N/A | Emitted as a standard `WATCH` request. | Missing `audit-ID` field in the starting log entry, making it impossible to correlate with other logs. |
| **Access log** | Reports the `LIST` method with accurate latency. | Reported as `WATCH` after session completion. Latency reflects the entire session. | No specific log entry for the completion of the *initial listing* phase. `apf_init_latency` excludes serialization time. |
| **Trace log** | Detailed logs for each step exceeding 500ms. | No trace logs generated due to streaming processing. | Missing traces that can break down streamed response writing into Network, Compress, and Serialize stages. |
| **Latency metric**| `apiserver_request_duration_seconds` (STABLE). | `apiserver_watch_list_duration_seconds` (ALPHA). | Metric needs to reach **BETA** stability for reliable production use. |


Subtasks:
* https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/136084
* https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/136085
* https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/136086
* Tracing WatchList
  * Add trace logs for WatchList
  * https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/136002
  * Add timings into audit logs

### Why is this needed?

With Kubernetes 1.35, controllers will switch to WatchList by default. Without these improvements, performance debugging for large clusters (e.g., clusters with >40,000 nodes) becomes nearly impossible, as we lose the ability to determine if a slow initial sync is caused by serialization, compression, or network bottlenecks.

### **Example Logs for Reference**

#### **LIST Method (Legacy)**
**Access log:**
```text
I0102 09:50:22.075010 1 httplog.go:134] "HTTP" verb="LIST" URI="/api/v1/pods?limit=500&resourceVersion=0" latency="4.508309573s" userAgent="kube-controller-manager/v1.36.0" audit-ID="c736a782-3402-4072-8a25-c17f89598189" resp=200
```

**Trace log:**
```text
Trace[1456271344]: "List" audit-id:c736a782-3402-4072-8a25-c17f89598189,verb:LIST (total time: 4507ms):
Trace[1456271344]: ["cacher.GetList" 4507ms]
Trace[1456271344]: ["SerializeObject" 4502ms]
```

#### **WatchList Method (Current Gaps)**
**Initial log (Missing correlation):**
```text
I0102 09:37:41.848779 1 get.go:263] "Starting watch" path="/api/v1/pods" resourceVersion=""
```

**Access log (Latency reflects total session, not just initial listing):**
```text
I0102 09:39:25.708328 1 httplog.go:134] "HTTP" verb="WATCH" URI="/api/v1/pods?sendInitialEvents=true..." latency="1m43.859s" audit-ID="2f3e2ef1-e9ed-49ed-8963-65ddc0c50244" apf_init_latency="3.985s" resp=200
```


/sig api-machinery
/sig instrumentation
/sig scalability
/cc @p0lyn0mial @wojtek-t @jpbetz @liggitt @richabanker
kubernetes/kubernetes#136003.title: Improve WatchList Observability: Addressing Logging, Tracing, and Metric Gaps

kubernetes/kubernetes#136027.body:
Currently, some components in Kubernetes have been migrated (or partially migrated) to [contextual logging](https://github.com/kubernetes/enhancements/blob/master/keps/sig-instrumentation/3077-contextual-logging/README.md). However, an incorrect pattern was introduced during the migration: Error-level logs are being called with verbosity methods (e.g., .V()).

The underlying package go-logr/logr, which contextual logging relies on, ignores the verbosity level when printing Error-level logs. For example, in the statement `logger.V(8).Error(nil, "Readiness probe already exists for container", "pod", klog.KObj(pod), "containerName", c.Name)`, the error log will be printed regardless of the verbosity level set.

This can lead to error log flooding, which may hinder users' ability to review normal logs for troubleshooting. More critically, according to the [Kubernetes logging policy](https://github.com/kubernetes/community/blob/master/contributors/devel/sig-instrumentation/logging.md#what-method-to-use), logs should only be recorded when the information requires action from an administrator. This directly contradicts the practice of setting verbosity levels for Error logs, as administrators using the default logging level might miss these actionable errors. Therefore, these instances need to be fixed.

Using grep, I identified the following instances in the k/k repository where verbosity levels are incorrectly set for Error logs.
- [ ] /pkg/controller/garbagecollector https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/136040
```
./pkg/controller/garbagecollector/garbagecollector.go:363:			logger.V(5).Error(err, "error syncing item", "item", n.identity)
./pkg/controller/garbagecollector/graph_builder.go:225:		logger.V(4).Error(err, "unable to use a shared informer", "resource", resource, "kind", kind)
```
- [ ] /pkg/controller/resourcequota https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/136040
```
./pkg/controller/resourcequota/resource_quota_monitor.go:178:	logger.V(4).Error(err, "QuotaMonitor unable to use a shared informer", "resource", resource.String())
```
- [x] /pkg/controller/storageversionmigrator/ https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/136046
```
./pkg/controller/storageversionmigrator/storageversionmigrator.go:227:		logger.V(4).Error(err, "resource does not exist in our rest mapper", "gvr", gvr.String())
./pkg/controller/storageversionmigrator/storageversionmigrator.go:244:		logger.V(4).Error(errMonitor, "resource does not exist in GC", "gvr", gvr.String())
./pkg/controller/storageversionmigrator/storageversionmigrator.go:311:			logger.V(4).Error(err, "Unable to compare the resource version of the resource", "namespace", accessor.GetNamespace(), "name", accessor.GetName(), "gvr", gvr.String(), "accessorRV", accessor.GetResourceVersion(), "listResourceVersion", listResourceVersion, "error", err.Error())
./pkg/controller/storageversionmigrator/storageversionmigrator.go:361:			logger.V(4).Error(errPatch, "Failed to migrate the resource", "namespace", accessor.GetNamespace(), "name", accessor.GetName(), "gvr", gvr.String(), "reason", apierrors.ReasonForError(errPatch))
```
- [ ] ./pkg/controller/volume/ https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/136050
```
./pkg/controller/volume/selinuxwarning/selinux_warning_controller.go:458:			logger.V(4).Error(err, "failed to get SELinux label", "pod", klog.KObj(pod), "volume", mount)
./pkg/controller/volume/selinuxwarning/selinux_warning_controller.go:514:			logger.V(2).Error(err, "failed to get first pod for event", "pod", conflict.Pod)
```
- [ ] ./pkg/kubelet/volumemanager/  https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/136033
```

```

kubernetes/kubernetes#136049.body:
### What happened?

Goroutine 1:If the method distribute() is invoked first, a goroutine holds the **RLock()** and calls listener.add(obj).
https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/f36be36c76192c9ccaf8c22d61ec9ed3c0a2f978/staging/src/k8s.io/client-go/tools/cache/shared_informer.go#L863-L874
The goroutine get blocked at the `p.addCh <- notification `
https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/f36be36c76192c9ccaf8c22d61ec9ed3c0a2f978/staging/src/k8s.io/client-go/tools/cache/shared_informer.go#L1015-L1020

Goroutine 2:After that, The method shouldResync() is invoked, and the goroutine get blocked at `p.resyncLock.Lock()` , waiting for the **RLock()** to be released.
https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/f36be36c76192c9ccaf8c22d61ec9ed3c0a2f978/staging/src/k8s.io/client-go/tools/cache/shared_informer.go#L1090-L1099

Goroutine 3: Because the goroutine 2 tries to get Lock() and Write lock has a higher priority. So p.listenersLock.RLock() get blocked, which results in p.wg.Start(listener.pop) can't get executed.
https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/f36be36c76192c9ccaf8c22d61ec9ed3c0a2f978/staging/src/k8s.io/client-go/tools/cache/shared_informer.go#L881-L890

And the `<-p.addCh` in listener.pop() can't be executed.

https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/f36be36c76192c9ccaf8c22d61ec9ed3c0a2f978/staging/src/k8s.io/client-go/tools/cache/shared_informer.go#L1037

In the situation, these three goroutines are all blocked. The interleaving sequence that triggers the bug is as follows:
```
G1                                            G2                             G3
p.resyncLock.RLock()
p.addCh <- notification 
                                            p.resyncLock.Lock()
                                                                               p.resyncLock.RLock()                                 
                                                                                <-p.addCh                  
```

### What did you expect to happen?

No goroutines leak.

### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

1. Adding time.Sleep(time.Second * 7) before `p.listenersLock.RLock()`. This step is to ensure that Lock is executed before RLock.
https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/f36be36c76192c9ccaf8c22d61ec9ed3c0a2f978/staging/src/k8s.io/client-go/tools/cache/shared_informer.go#L881-L883

```
time.Sleep(time.Second * 7)
p.listenersLock.RLock()
```
2. Using goleak to detect the bug in the test function.
https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/f36be36c76192c9ccaf8c22d61ec9ed3c0a2f978/staging/src/k8s.io/client-go/tools/cache/shared_informer_test.go#L210

```
func TestListenerResyncPeriods(t *testing.T) {
	defer goleak.VerifyNone(t)
```

The bug report is as follows,

```

kubernetes/kubernetes#136107.body:
### What would you like to be added?

Before introduction of Beta metric stability, we had many important metrics that were stuck in Alpha state.

Would like to propose a list of metric candidates that should be reviewed for graduation to Beta.

We would still review each metric individually to any antipattern, but fact they are in use for so long should be good enough reason for graduation. 

Graduation criteria are at https://github.com/kubernetes/community/blob/9de615daee15bdee370a825dc0ef150eac189700/contributors/devel/sig-instrumentation/metric-instrumentation.md#graduating-to-beta

Subtasks:
* https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/136304
* https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/136305
* https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/136306
* https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/136307
* https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/136308
* https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/136310
* https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/136311
* https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/136312
* https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/136313


### Why is this needed?

Those metrics have been already present in K8s for long time and are at use by GKE. 

While we won't necessarily want to graduate them GA, marking them as Beta would indicate their importance and allow community to pay more attention to them over other Alpha metrics.

/cc @richabanker @dgrisonnet
kubernetes/kubernetes#136107.title: Graduate old Alpha metrics to Beta

kubernetes/kubernetes#136135.body:
### What would you like to be added?

The HPA currently exposes several metrics that are still marked as alpha, even though they have met the [requirements for promotion to beta](https://github.com/kubernetes/community/blob/9de615daee15bdee370a825dc0ef150eac189700/contributors/devel/sig-instrumentation/metric-instrumentation.md#graduating-to-beta). Given their maturity and longevity, I believe the following metrics should be promoted to beta:

- `reconciliations_total` (introduced in #116010)
- `reconciliation_duration_seconds` (introduced in #116010)
- `metric_computation_total` (introduced in #116326)
- `metric_computation_duration_seconds` (introduced in #116326)

All of the metrics listed above have sufficient test coverage and have existed for a long time without significant issues, satisfying the criteria for promotion to beta.

### Why is this needed?

Ref: #135726

/assign
/cc @adrianmoisey @richabanker @dgrisonnet
kubernetes/kubernetes#136135.title: Promote old HPA alpha metrics to Beta

kubernetes/kubernetes#136201.body:
### What happened?

## Problem origin (ARO/OCP bugs)
- OCPBUGS-64883 and OCPBUGS-67200: on ARO with UDR and a non-responsive DNS, new workers never register Node/CSINode. Kubelet restarts during CSI init.

## Root cause
- NSS `hosts:` ordering on RHEL/RHCOS/Fedora is typically `files dns myhostname`. With blackholed DNS, glibc waits for DNS timeouts before reaching `myhostname`.
- Default resolver budget can be ~20–60s (A+AAAA, up to 3 nameservers, attempts=2, timeout=5s), longer if `timeout`/`attempts` are higher.
- Kubelet’s CSI init backoff in `pkg/volume/csi/csi_plugin.go` is ~23s total, despite the comment claiming ~140s. It exits before DNS fallback completes, so kubelet restarts and never initializes `CSINode`.

## Why increase the timeout
- To survive realistic DNS timeouts and let `myhostname` succeed, the backoff should exceed the DNS worst case. Raising it toward the documented ~140s (or at least >60–90s) aligns behavior with the comment and prevents restart loops when DNS is slow/unreachable.

## OS/NSS considerations
- Reordering NSS to place `myhostname` before `dns` would avoid the wait, but distro defaults/operator preferences differ; consensus is hard. Increasing kubelet’s backoff is a practical, compatible mitigation.

## Proposed backoff
```
New exponential backoff steps:
  sleep 1:  32.55437ms
  sleep 2:  254.104702ms
  sleep 3:  2.059965819s
  sleep 4:  16.567802964s
  sleep 5:  2m14.365124124s  <-- roughly 140 seconds
```

## Request
- Update CSI init backoff parameters so the actual cap matches the intended ~140s (or another explicit target ≥ DNS timeout budget), and fix the comment to reflect the real value. This allows kubelet to complete CSI/CSINode initialization even when DNS is slow or unreachable.

### What did you expect to happen?

Exponential backoff should be roughly 140 seconds.

### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

    1. Create a new worker node whose DNS server will time out
    2. Observe that kubelet will never create the Node object

### Anything else we need to know?

NOTE: I assume that the original backoff values assumed there will be 6 sleep cycles. However, the `ExponentialBackoff` skips the last sleep iteration: https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/9f6977db54084d766c1fec19f383bf509d7e8610/staging/src/k8s.io/apimachinery/pkg/util/wait/backoff.go#L480-L481

### Kubernetes version

<details>

```console
Reproduced on Kubernetes 1.33 (from original bug report - OpenShift 4.20)
```

</details>


### Cloud provider

<details>
ARO
</details>


### OS version

<details>

```console
N/A
```

</details>


### Install tools

<details>

</details>

kubernetes/kubernetes#136228.body:
### What happened?

Hello,

I've been debugging issues with my Kubernetes nodes having very high CPU usage in network components.

I've finally traced down the origin to that block of code, which is overriding sysctl settings for iptables with insanely high values.
https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/master/pkg/proxy/conntrack/sysctls.go#L62

The default is for the kernel to configurable iptables data structures based on available memory. Typically 32k-256k buckets and 256k max entries (this may be a tad low for the most busy servers). 

**Problem: Kubernetes is reconfiguring the iptables data structures based on the number of CPU cores. It's broken on machines with many CPU.
On my servers with 512 cores, I am seeing Kubernetes reconfigure the kernel to have 16M iptables entries with a 4M hash table.
That's a 32 MB hash table that can't fit in CPU cache, with 6 GB of entries. It's ridiculous.**

The large data structure is causing constant thrashing of the CPU cache on all cores, as every network packet has to lookup the table multiple times, and network packets are distributed across all CPU cores for processing.

Some operations need to go over the whole hashtable, for read or write (write has locks), which is affected by the large size of the table. This is why you've been having issues with nginx proxy (among other bugs), you've got bug tickets investigating performance issue with processing/updating that table, but nobody pointing out that it should not have millions of items, no wonder it's slow to process!

Can you please correct this code?

This should be set to a fixed value. I think 256k buckets, 1M max entries is a good default value that should work for >99% of users.
If anyone reaches that limit, they should check with `netstat` what is consuming active entries, then they probably need to reduce the sysctl timeout to evict these entries faster, as the default is keeping them for too long.

```
net.nf_conntrack_max = 1048576
net.netfilter.nf_conntrack_buckets = 262144
```

Regards.

### What did you expect to happen?

iptables should have reasonable configuration

### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

you can look at sysctl settings on any kubernetes nodes with many cores and see the problem

### Anything else we need to know?

_No response_

### Kubernetes version

all versions affected

<details>

```console
$ kubectl version
# paste output here
```

</details>


### Cloud provider

<details>
on premise
</details>


### OS version

all Linux OS are affected as kubernetes is incorrected overwriting iptables settings from the OS.

# paste output here
```

kubernetes/kubernetes#136239.body:
new: add test and log for kubelet (Kubernetes v1.35.0).

### What happened?

For Pods using Dynamic Resource Allocation (DRA), the kubelet status manager continuously logs "Pod status is inconsistent with cached status" and triggers a reconciliation loop (needsReconcile -> syncPod, every ~10s). This loop is unnecessary because no actual status changes are applied, but it adds avoidable load on kubelet, API server, and etcd.
kubelet log -v=4
```
I0115 16:58:36.998365  584662 status_manager.go:262] "Syncing all statuses"
I0115 16:58:36.999471  584662 status_manager.go:1175] "Pod status is inconsistent with cached status for pod, a reconciliation should be triggered" pod="default/dp-e7c13e09e5-5b4fd7bb6d-f95kc" statusDiff=<
	@@ -183,11 +183,5 @@
	    }
	   }
	  ],
	- "qosClass": "Guaranteed",
	- "resourceClaimStatuses": [
	-  {
	-   "name": "test-kubelet",
	-   "resourceClaimName": "dp-e7c13e09e5-5b4fd7bb6d-f95kc-test-kubelet-nh64j"
	-  }
	- ]
	+ "qosClass": "Guaranteed"
	 }
 >
I0115 16:58:36.999523  584662 kubelet_pods.go:1223] "Clean up pod workers for terminated pods"
......
I0115 16:58:37.002499  584662 status_manager.go:1065] "Patch status for pod" pod="default/dp-e7c13e09e5-5b4fd7bb6d-f95kc" podUID="d949fbdc-23b5-4a50-b57b-31ea53189032" patch="{\"metadata\":{\"uid\":\"d949fbdc-23b5-4a50-b57b-31ea53189032\"}}"
I0115 16:58:37.002510  584662 status_manager.go:1072] "Status for pod is up-to-date" pod="default/dp-e7c13e09e5-5b4fd7bb6d-f95kc" statusVersion=2
......
I0115 16:58:46.998609  584662 status_manager.go:262] "Syncing all statuses"
I0115 16:58:46.998627  584662 kubelet.go:2685] "SyncLoop (housekeeping)"
I0115 16:58:46.999152  584662 status_manager.go:1175] "Pod status is inconsistent with cached status for pod, a reconciliation should be triggered" pod="default/dp-e7c13e09e5-5b4fd7bb6d-f95kc" statusDiff=<
	@@ -183,11 +183,5 @@
	    }
	   }
	  ],
	- "qosClass": "Guaranteed",
	- "resourceClaimStatuses": [
	-  {
	-   "name": "test-kubelet",
	-   "resourceClaimName": "dp-e7c13e09e5-5b4fd7bb6d-f95kc-test-kubelet-nh64j"
	-  }
	- ]
	+ "qosClass": "Guaranteed"
	 }
 >
```


### What did you expect to happen?

The kubelet should ignore non-kubelet-owned fields (ResourceClaimStatuses, ExtendedResourceClaimStatus) during status comparison, and not trigger unnecessary reconciliation loops for DRA Pods.


### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

1. Deploy a Kubernetes cluster with DRA enabled.
2. Create a Pod that uses DRA resources (with ResourceClaim).
3. Observe the kubelet logs and API server/etcd load.
4. Find the continuous "status inconsistent" logs and repeated reconciliation operations.

### Anything else we need to know?

This issue is caused by the `isPodStatusByKubeletEqual` function comparing the entire Pod status, including fields that are not managed by kubelet (populated by control plane components). A fix is provided in PR #136238.

kubernetes/kubernetes#136240.body:
### What happened?

When creating a Service with ipFamilyPolicy: RequireDualStack or PreferredDualstack on Windows nodes, kube-proxy sometimes programs incorrect load balancers.

The issue manifests in two main ways:

1. Incorrect backend selection
    - IPv6 endpoints are sometimes programmed as backends for IPv4 VIPs
    - IPv4 endpoints are sometimes programmed as backends for IPv6 VIPs
    - This results in traffic blackholing or connection failures

2. Incorrect load balancer flags
   - IPv6-specific HNS flags are sometimes applied to IPv4 load balancers
   - IPv4 flags are sometimes applied to IPv6 load balancers
   - This causes inconsistent behavior and unexpected failures in datapath programming

These issues appear intermittent and are more frequently observed under:

- Endpoint updates
- Service updates
- Rapid pod churn 

### What did you expect to happen?

For a RequireDualStack Service:

- IPv4 VIPs should:
    - Use only IPv4 endpoints
    - Be programmed with IPv4-specific HNS flags

- IPv6 VIPs should:
    - Use only IPv6 endpoints
    - Be programmed with IPv6-specific HNS flags

The programming behavior should be deterministic and consistent, matching the dual-stack service semantics defined in the Kubernetes dual-stack KEP.

### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

1. Create a Windows cluster with:
    - Dual-stack networking enabled
    - Windows nodes using winkernel kube-proxy
2. Create a Service:
 
```
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: test-dualstack-lb
spec:
  type: LoadBalancer
  ipFamilyPolicy: RequireDualStack
  ipFamilies:
    - IPv4
    - IPv6
  selector:
    app: test
  ports:
    - port: 80
      targetPort: 80
```
 
3. Deploy dual-stack pods with both IPv4 and IPv6 addresses
4. Trigger updates:
    - Scale pods up/down
    - Restart kube-proxy
    - Update endpoints or service annotations
5. Observe HNS load balancers and backend programming

### Anything else we need to know?

- This issue appears to be specific to Windows kube-proxy (winkernel).
- This issue violates the dual-stack contract defined in the Kubernetes dual-stack KEP.

### Kubernetes version

<details>

```console
All K8S versions
```

kubernetes/kubernetes#136288.body:
### What happened?

When upgrading a Kubernetes cluster from v1.32 to v1.33 by creating new control plane nodes with v1.33 manifests, the kube-apiserver fails to start with admission errors. 
```
0115 20:56:16.106534   # log start
...
E0115 20:56:24.741991       1 repairip.go:372] "Unhandled Error" err="the ClusterIP [IPv4]: 10.211.95.185 for Service <namespace foo>/<service bar>is not allocated; repairing" logger="UnhandledError"
...
Trace[295533714]: ---"Write to database call failed" len:247,err:ipaddresses.networking.k8s.io "10.211.95.185" is forbidden: not yet ready to handle request 10000ms (20:56:34.744)
Trace[295533714]: [10.000527316s] [10.000527316s] END
E0115 20:56:34.745027       1 repairip.go:235] "Unhandled Error" err="ipaddresses.networking.k8s.io \"10.211.95.185\" is forbidden: not yet ready to handle request" logger="UnhandledError"
I0115 20:56:34.746095       1 repairip.go:236] Shutting down ipallocator-repair-controller
I0115 20:56:34.834949       1 healthz.go:280] informer-sync,poststarthook/start-service-ip-repair-controllers check failed: readyz
[-]informer-sync failed: 4 informers not started yet: [*v1.Pod *v1.ServiceAccount *v1.Secret *v1.Namespace]
[-]poststarthook/start-service-ip-repair-controllers failed: not finished
...
I0115 20:57:06.335471       1 healthz.go:280] informer-sync,poststarthook/start-service-ip-repair-controllers check failed: readyz
[-]informer-sync failed: 1 informers not started yet: [*v1.Secret] # v1.Namespace informer is ready  
[-]poststarthook/start-service-ip-repair-controllers failed: not finished
...
F0115 20:57:18.630248       1 hooks.go:204] PostStartHook "start-service-ip-repair-controllers" failed: unable to perform initial IP and Port allocation check

```

We think this is a race condition introduced by new code path when `MultiCIDRServiceAllocator` is enabled by default in v1.33.  
`start-service-ip-repair-controllers` reads clusterIPs from all `Services` objects and creates `IPAddresses` objects if they are not found from cache. Since we are upgrading from v1.32, where `MultiCIDRServiceAllocator` is disabled, ETCD does not contain any `IPAddresses` objects. 
`start-service-ip-repair-controllers` therefore does not read any existing `IPAddresses` objects from cache and attempts to create them. However, at this point, the `ipaddresses.networking.k8s.io` API is not yet ready. As a result, `start-service-ip-repair-controllers` failes and the kube-apiserver never becomes ready.

Because we have a large number of namespaces, `v1.Namespace` informer takes more time to become ready, which increases the likelihood of this issue occurring in our cluster.
In v1.32 `MultiCIDRServiceAllocator` is disabled. The `start-service-ip-repair-controllers`  implementation only invokes the internal `RangeRegistry` API, which is initialized earlier, so this issue did not exist.



### What did you expect to happen?

The kube-apiserver should start successfully and the repair controller should create missing `IPAddress` objects for existing `Services`, either by:
1. Waiting for admission plugins to be ready before attempting writes
2. Deferring repair work until after startup completes
3. Retrying failed operations instead of terminating

### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

Prerequisites:
- Cluster with large number of `Namespaces` and `Services` 
- `MultiCIDRServiceAllocator` feature gate previously disabled

Steps:
1. Prepare v1.33 kube-apiserver manifests with `MultiCIDRServiceAllocator` enabled by default
2. Start kube-apiserver
3. Observe API server fails to start


### Anything else we need to know?

_No response_

### Kubernetes version

<details>

```console
control-plane components: v1.33.7
worker node kubelet: v1.30.7
kube-proxy: v1.31.11
```

</details>


### Cloud provider

<details>
Self-managed cluster on AWS ec2 instances

</details>

### OS version

<details>

```console
# On Linux:
$ cat /etc/os-release
PRETTY_NAME="Ubuntu 24.04.3 LTS"
NAME="Ubuntu"
VERSION_ID="24.04"
VERSION="24.04.3 LTS (Noble Numbat)"
VERSION_CODENAME=noble
ID=ubuntu
ID_LIKE=debian
HOME_URL="https://www.ubuntu.com/"
SUPPORT_URL="https://help.ubuntu.com/"
BUG_REPORT_URL="https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/"
PRIVACY_POLICY_URL="https://www.ubuntu.com/legal/terms-and-policies/privacy-policy"
UBUNTU_CODENAME=noble
LOGO=ubuntu-logo
$ uname -a
Linux ip-10-206-19-65 6.8.0-1044-aws #46-Ubuntu SMP Fri Nov 21 16:50:44 UTC 2025 x86_64 x86_64 x86_64 GNU/Linux

```

</details>

kubernetes/kubernetes#136323.body:
### What happened? After upgrading our Kubernetes cluster from v1.33.5 to v1.34.2 (AWS EKS), pods scheduled on nodes running v1.34.2 fail during container creation with the following error: ```bash grpc: error while marshaling: string field contains invalid UTF-8 ``` The pods are created successfully, but containers fail to create. Important observation: Pods scheduled on nodes still running v1.33.5 continue to work correctly with the same manifests and environment variables. The failure occurs only on nodes upgraded to v1.34.2, strongly indicating a regression. Through isolation and testing, we observed that the failure occurs when an environment variable value contains a $ immediately followed by a non-ASCII character. This behaviour did not occur on v1.33.5 and appears to be a regression introduced in v1.34.x. ### What did you expect to happen? Pods and containers should start successfully when environment variable values contain valid UTF-8 characters, including non-ASCII characters and literal $ characters. This configuration worked as expected on v1.33.5 nodes, even within the same cluster during a rolling upgrade. ### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)? 1. Create a pod (or deployment) with an environment variable containing: - a $ - immediately followed by a non-ASCII UTF-8 character 3. Schedule the pod on a v1.34.2 node 4. Observe container creation failure with: - `grpc: error while marshaling: string field contains invalid UTF-8` Minimal reproducible example: ```yaml apiVersion: v1 kind: Pod metadata: name: utf8-env-repro spec: containers: - name: test image: busybox command: ["sh", "-c", "echo started && sleep 3600"] env: - name: TEST_ENV value: "$£FOO" ``` Observed result: ❌ Fails on v1.34.2 nodes ✅ Works on v1.33.5 nodes Expected result: Container starts normally on all versions. ### Anything else we need to know? - We run a custom CRD/operator that propagates env vars from the CR spec into Deployments and multi-container Pods. - The failure occurs after Pod creation, during container creation / runtime interaction. - This reproduces across all containers in the pod that receive the env var. - This is reproducible in a mixed-version cluster: - Pods on v1.33.5 nodes work - Pods on v1.34.2 nodes fail ### Kubernetes version
```console $ kubectl version Client Version: v1.32.0 Kustomize Version: v5.5.0 Server Version: v1.34.2-eks-b3126f4 WARNING: version difference between client (1.32) and server (1.34) exceeds the supported minor version skew of +/-1 ```
### Cloud provider
AWS EKS Cluster upgrade from v1.33.5 → v1.34.2
### OS version
```console # On Linux: $ cat /etc/os-release NAME="Amazon Linux" VERSION="2023" ID="amzn" ID_LIKE="fedora" VERSION_ID="2023" PLATFORM_ID="platform:al2023" PRETTY_NAME="Amazon Linux 2023.9.20251208" ANSI_COLOR="0;33" CPE_NAME="cpe:2.3:o:amazon:amazon_linux:2023" HOME_URL="https://aws.amazon.com/linux/amazon-linux-2023/" DOCUMENTATION_URL="https://docs.aws.amazon.com/linux/" SUPPORT_URL="https://aws.amazon.com/premiumsupport/" BUG_REPORT_URL="https://github.com/amazonlinux/amazon-linux-2023" VENDOR_NAME="AWS" VENDOR_URL="https://aws.amazon.com/" SUPPORT_END="2029-06-30" $ uname -a Linux ip-10-11-156-253.eu-central-1.compute.internal 6.12.58-82.121.amzn2023.x86_64 #1 SMP PREEMPT_DYNAMIC Thu Nov 27 09:07:41 UTC 2025 x86_64 x86_64 x86_64 GNU/Linux # On Windows: C:\> wmic os get Caption, Version, BuildNumber, OSArchitecture # paste output here ```
