kubernetes/kubernetes#136333.body:
### What happened?

In Kubernetes 1.34, the KubeletPSI feature gate was set to true. However, when running Kubernetes on an OS that does not have PSI enabled, the PSI metrics are not expected to be generated. Here are the counts of the metrics observed during a test:
```
# for node in $(kubectl get nodes -o jsonpath='{.items[*].metadata.name}'); do echo "=== Node: $node ==="; for metric in cpu_waiting cpu_stalled memory_waiting memory_stalled io_waiting io_stalled; do echo -n "container_pressure_${metric}_seconds_total: "; kubectl get --raw "/api/v1/nodes/$node/proxy/metrics/cadvisor" | grep "container_pressure_${metric}_seconds_total" | wc -l; done; done
=== Node: ip-10-0-11-217.us-east-2.compute.internal ===
container_pressure_cpu_waiting_seconds_total:      267
container_pressure_cpu_stalled_seconds_total:      267
container_pressure_memory_waiting_seconds_total:      267
container_pressure_memory_stalled_seconds_total:      267
container_pressure_io_waiting_seconds_total:      267
container_pressure_io_stalled_seconds_total:      267
```
I suspect this part of the code:

https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/master/pkg/kubelet/cadvisor/cadvisor_linux.go#L105C53-L105C63
```
	if utilfeature.DefaultFeatureGate.Enabled(features.KubeletPSI) {
		includedMetrics[cadvisormetrics.PressureMetrics] = struct{}{}
	} 
```
and prometheus does this: https://github.com/google/cadvisor/blob/master/metrics/prometheus.go#L1842
```
	if includedMetrics.Has(container.PressureMetrics) {
		c.containerMetrics = append(c.containerMetrics, []containerMetric{
			{
				name:      "container_pressure_cpu_stalled_seconds_total", 
```

cc: @haircommander @bitoku 

### What did you expect to happen?

There should be no PSI metrics when underlying OS never enables it.

### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

Run k8s 1.34 and above and use grafana to monitor PSI metrics

### Anything else we need to know?

This is a good to have. Doesn't really impact anything as cardinality is also negligible. This zero value confuses the end-user as it gives a visual impression that PSI is enabled when looking at grafana charts.

I can propose a fix on this.

1.34

kubernetes/kubernetes#136415.body:
https://github.com/google/btree is archived.

Based on the discussion here - https://github.com/etcd-io/etcd/issues/20991, we have implemented the btree in `k8s/utils`. The same needs to be referenced in all instances. 

Currently there is only one instance in apiserver, which needs changes.
kubernetes/kubernetes#136415.title: Replace deprecated `https://github.com/google/btree`

kubernetes/kubernetes#136460.body:
### What happened?

Several kubectl resource describers don't have corresponding tests in `pkg/describe/describe_test.go`.
https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/master/staging/src/k8s.io/kubectl/pkg/describe/describe_test.go

### What did you expect to happen?

These describers should have basic test to verify their output formatting and prevent regressions.
The following describers have implementations but no tests:

- `ReplicaSet`
- `CronJob`
- `Role`
- `ClusterRole`
- `RoleBinding`
- `ClusterRoleBinding`
- `CertificateSigningRequest`


### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

Check `pkg/describe/describe_test.go`. You won't find test functions like `TestDescribeReplicaSet`, `TestDescribeCronJob`, etc. for the listed resources.

### Anything else we need to know?

I checked describers registered in the describer and found these are missing tests. I excluded deprecated resources (`Endpoints`, `ReplicationController` which don't have any test in the same way) and resources already covered under different API groups (`ServiceCIDR`, `IPAddress` under `networkingv1beta1`).


### Kubernetes version

<details>

```console
$ kubectl version
# paste output here
```

</details>


### Cloud provider

<details>

</details>


### OS version

<details>

```console
# On Linux:
$ cat /etc/os-release
# paste output here
$ uname -a
# paste output here

# On Windows:
C:\> wmic os get Caption, Version, BuildNumber, OSArchitecture
# paste output here
```

</details>


### Install tools

<details>

</details>


### Container runtime (CRI) and version (if applicable)

<details>

</details>


### Related plugins (CNI, CSI, ...) and versions (if applicable)

<details>

</details>

kubernetes/kubernetes#136500.body:
#### What type of PR is this?

Follow-up to #136292
@pohly suggested a set of rules from [modernize](https://pkg.go.dev/golang.org/x/tools/go/analysis/passes/modernize) that should be enabled as PR hints.

I've experimented with `slicescontains` which finds many matches across the entire code-base, and can be used to simplify code. There are some cases of internal functions that can be eliminated in favor a single `slices.Contains`:

* `func has(...`
* `func contains(...` (there are some variations of this).
* `func containsStr(...` 

/kind cleanup

#### What this PR does / why we need it:

It's good to use newer features in GoLang, especially when it serves to simplify the code. One highlight is that these rules should remain as hints, till they can be safely enabled.

#### Which issue(s) this PR is related to:

#### Special notes for your reviewer:

I don't know which is the correct way to write things in the hints golangci-lint file. `Hints` instead of `Base` didn't yield different files with `./hack/update-golangci-lint-config.sh`

#### Does this PR introduce a user-facing change?

```release-note
NONE
```
#### Additional documentation e.g., KEPs (Kubernetes Enhancement Proposals), usage docs, etc.:

kubernetes/kubernetes#136527.body:
### What happened?

I encountered a confusing validation error while trying to update the `.status.startTime` of an existing  
Job.
The error message received was:
```bash
Job.batch "job-c4nbt" is invalid: status.startTime: Required value: startTime cannot be removed for unsuspended job
```
However, I am not trying to remove the `startTime`, but rather update it. I checked the code and found that the validation logic triggers this error for any inequality, not just when the `startTime` is removed (set to nil).

https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/f4eedc41b8c7aa9a4c66c08d153d8d4ce6c1e6a6/pkg/apis/batch/validation/validation.go#L740-L746

### What did you expect to happen?

I expected the error message to reflect the actual validation constraint accurately.

### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

1. create a non-suspended Job
2. update `job.status.startTime` to a different value using:
```go
    kubeClient.BatchV1().Jobs().UpdateStatus()
```

### Anything else we need to know?

If this is confirmed as an issue, I would be happy to submit a PR to correct the error message and potentially the error type to make it more user-friendly.

kubernetes/kubernetes#136545.body:
## What would you like to be added?

Add a `tlsServerName` field to the `TLSConfig` structure in `EgressSelectorConfiguration` to allow overriding the hostname used for TLS certificate verification, similar to the existing `tls-server-name` field in kubeconfig (added in[ #88769](https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/88769)).

## Precedent

This functionality already exists in:
- **kubeconfig files**: The `tls-server-name` field was added in #88769 for similar use cases
- **kubectl**: The `--tls-server-name` flag provides command-line access
- **Go's `tls.Config`**: The underlying `ServerName` field supports this

The egress selector `TLSConfig` would benefit from the same capability for consistency.

## Why is this needed?

### Use Case

This addresses scenarios where components using EgressSelectorConfiguration connect through Kubernetes Service DNS names (e.g., proxy.svc.cluster.local) but that hostname is not included in the server certificate's Subject Alternative Names (SANs), which define which hostnames a certificate is valid for.

**Example scenario:**
- Egress selector is configured to connect to: `https://konnectivity-service.example.svc.cluster.local:8443`
- The proxy's TLS certificate contains SANs for: `proxy.example.com`
- Connection fails because `konnectivity-service.example.svc.cluster.local` is not in the certificate SANs

### Current Workaround Limitations

The current workaround of updating certificates to include all possible service DNS names has significant drawbacks:
- Requires certificate regeneration and rotation across many clusters
- Service names may vary across environments (dev, staging, prod)
- Certificate management becomes complex in multi-tenant environments
- May require coordination with external PKI infrastructure

### Proposed Solution

Add an optional `tlsServerName` field to override the hostname used for certificate verification:

```yaml
apiVersion: apiserver.k8s.io/v1beta1
kind: EgressSelectorConfiguration
egressSelections:
- name: cluster
  connection:
    proxyProtocol: HTTPConnect
    transport:
      tcp:
        url: "https://proxy-service.example.svc.cluster.local:8443"
        tlsConfig:
          caBundle: /etc/kubernetes/pki/ca.crt
          clientCert: /etc/kubernetes/pki/client.crt
          clientKey: /etc/kubernetes/pki/client.key
          tlsServerName: "proxy.example.com"  # <-- New field
```
This would allow connecting via the service DNS name while verifying the certificate against the actual hostname in the SANs.

/sig api-machinery
/sig auth
/kind feature
kubernetes/kubernetes#136545.title: Support TLS Server Name overrides in EgressSelectorConfiguration

kubernetes/kubernetes#136563.body:
### What happened?

While auditing stale TODO comments in the GCE configuration scripts (following my recent merged cleanup PR #136379), I identified a 9-year-old TODO at line 525 of cluster/gce/config-test.sh. The environment variable KUBE_PROXY_DAEMONSET still defaults to false, causing kube-proxy to be deployed as static pods in certain GCE test configurations.

### What did you expect to happen?

I expect GCE cluster configurations to align with modern Kubernetes standards (like kubeadm), where kube-proxy is managed as a DaemonSet by default. This change will allow for the future removal of legacy static-pod logic in cluster/gce/gci/configure-helper.sh, significantly reducing technical debt.

### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

1. Inspect cluster/gce/config-test.sh at line 527.
2. Observe KUBE_PROXY_DAEMONSET=${KUBE_PROXY_DAEMONSET:-false}.
3. This stems from Commit 1102656 (PR #50705) from 2017, where the variable was introduced as an experimental option but never flipped to the default.

### Anything else we need to know?

I am a DevOps Engineer and am happy to take ownership of this transition. My plan is to:

1. Flip the default to true.
2. Monitor CI signal (pull-kubernetes-e2e-gce) to ensure no regressions.
3. Submit a follow-up PR to deprecate the false code path once verified.

### Kubernetes version

v1.36-alpha


### Cloud provider

GCP / GCE


### OS version

N/A - Script Logic Audit

### Install tools

kube-up.sh


### Container runtime (CRI) and version (if applicable)

containerd

### Related plugins (CNI, CSI, ...) and versions (if applicable)

kube-proxy

NewUnmounter can ignore errors for volume dir no-exists

These tests fail:

sig-network said I should create an issue for that ([Slack Thread](https://kubernetes.slack.com/archives/C09QYUH5W/p1769693199795519))

kubernetes/kubernetes#136628.body:
### What would you like to be added?

Currently the order in which the DRA allocator considers devices during scheduling is non-deterministic. Since the DRA allocator uses a first-fit algorithm, the order in which devices are considered can impact both the time required to schedule pods and which devices are selected.

We want to let DRA drivers specify the order in which the allocator will consider devices. This is discussed in more detail in https://github.com/kubernetes/enhancements/issues/5773 and https://github.com/kubernetes/enhancements/pull/5794.

### Why is this needed?

This will allow faster scheduling and allocation of more appropriate devices.
kubernetes/kubernetes#136628.title: Let DRA drivers specify the order in which devices are considered during scheduling

kubernetes/kubernetes#136631.body:
### What would you like to be added?

Building with a more vanilla setcap image shouldn't require a shell in the image to run the command 


in `build/server-image/kube-apiserver/Dockerfile`

```sh
# We apply cap_net_bind_service so that kube-apiserver can be run as
# non-root and still listen on port less than 1024
RUN setcap cap_net_bind_service=+ep /${BINARY}
```

this command requires the `SETCAP_IMAGE` have a shell in order to:
1) RUN a command that isn't in the form of ["/usr/sbin/setcap", "..."] 
2) shell expand the `${BINARY}` argument


### Why is this needed?

By making this change you can eliminate the need of having a shell in the setcap image:


```diff
diff --git a/build/server-image/kube-apiserver/Dockerfile b/build/server-image/kube-apiserver/Dockerfile
index 27129d29bd9..acfe8c26f22 100644
--- a/build/server-image/kube-apiserver/Dockerfile
+++ b/build/server-image/kube-apiserver/Dockerfile
@@ -20,11 +20,11 @@ ARG SETCAP_IMAGE
 # to setup qemu for the builder.
 FROM --platform=linux/$BUILDARCH ${SETCAP_IMAGE}
 ARG BINARY
-COPY --chmod=755 ${BINARY} /${BINARY}
+COPY --chmod=755 ${BINARY} /set_cap_binary
 # We apply cap_net_bind_service so that kube-apiserver can be run as
 # non-root and still listen on port less than 1024
-RUN setcap cap_net_bind_service=+ep /${BINARY}
+RUN ["setcap", "cap_net_bind_service=+ep", "/set_cap_binary"]
 
 FROM --platform=linux/$TARGETARCH ${BASEIMAGE}
 ARG BINARY
-COPY --from=0 /${BINARY} /usr/local/bin/${BINARY}
+COPY --from=0 /set_cap_binary /usr/local/bin/${BINARY}
```
kubernetes/kubernetes#136631.title: setcap build image shouldn't require a shell

kubernetes/kubernetes#136724.body:
**What would you like to be added?**

We have a few options:

1. Introduce a kubelet-server started by using `httpServer.Serve(YourTLSConfig)` with a x509.Config wrapper to update TLS config periodically. 
2. Set `GetConfigForClient` in `TLSOptions.Config` when initializing TLSOptions for kubelet. For reloading CA, use a cache which either lazy loads the CA (and cert + key) from disk with a TTL OR Implement a separate process which periodically sync the cache.
3. Move kubelet towards using the [k8s.io/apiserver](http://k8s.io/apiserver%60) `SecureServing` structures to set up its TLS handler if possible 


**Why is this needed?**

It will enable CA rotation for kubelet clients without requiring kubelet restarts.

/cc @liggitt @ahmedtd @deads2k 
/sig auth
/kind feature
kubernetes/kubernetes#136724.title: Introduce a kubelet-server configuration that allows reloading ClientCA in TLSConfig

kubernetes/kubernetes#136742.body:
### What happened?

When a deployment with one replica in crashloop, the kube-proxy logs spams the log with below message instead of ignoring the endpoint.
```
Ignoring same-zone topology hints for service since no hints were provided for zone
```

A developer cluster that has many pods in crash loop, mostly with one replica ( since it's a developer environment ) caused sudden spike in kube-proxy log spamming to otel and impacted log ingestion after upgrading to 1.33.9.

### What did you expect to happen?

kube-proxy should ignore endpoint with below condition while calling `topologyModeFromHints`

```
  endpoints:
    conditions:
      ready: false
      serving: false
      terminating: false
```

### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

- Upgrade Kubernetes to 1.33.9
- Scale the deployment to 0
- Change the image to a non-existent one
- Let the pod to be in ImagePull error
- Check the kube-proxy logs and that will show below message

`Ignoring same-zone topology hints for service since no hints were provided for zone`

### Anything else we need to know?

_No response_

```

AWS

kubernetes/kubernetes#136786.body:
### What happened?

kube-proxy crashes repeatedly, causing the container network to not function.
```
E0205 20:24:21.385767       1 proxier.go:1217] "Failed to list existing sets" err="failed to run nft: signal: segmentation fault (core dumped)" ipFamily="IPv4"
E0205 20:24:22.721950       1 proxier.go:1788] "nftables sync failed" err="signal: segmentation fault (core dumped)" ipFamily="IPv4"
I0205 20:24:22.723136       1 proxier.go:1143] "Sync failed" ipFamily="IPv4" retryingTime="30s"
E0205 20:24:23.698591       1 proxier.go:1217] "Failed to list existing sets" err="failed to run nft: signal: segmentation fault (core dumped)" ipFamily="IPv6"
E0205 20:24:24.761438       1 proxier.go:1788] "nftables sync failed" err="signal: segmentation fault (core dumped)" ipFamily="IPv6"
I0205 20:24:24.761928       1 proxier.go:1143] "Sync failed" ipFamily="IPv6" retryingTime="30s"
```

### What did you expect to happen?

kube-proxy should correctly setup nftables rules.


### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

Run a container in the host namespace with a newer version of nftables (1.1.3+), for example Fedora 43. Add a nft set in the container:
```
nft add table test_table
nft add set test_table test_set '{ type ifname; elements = { "test" } }'
```

Then delete the kube-proxy pod on the host, and example the logs on the new pod.


### Anything else we need to know?

kube-proxy in nftables mode crashes repeatedly on hosts where newer versions of nftables (eg 1.1.3) have created sets in the same network namespace.

I found a fix for the crash in newer nftables here (it appears to be related to udata being present on newer nft sets):
https://git.netfilter.org/nftables/commit/?id=be737a1986bfee0ddea4bee7863dca0123a2bcbc

It's probably a good idea to build kube-proxy with a patched version of nftables to avoid the crash, but not use anything newer than nftables 1.1.1 as it could then potentially break other unpatched nftables in CNIs etc.


### Kubernetes version

<details>

```console
Client Version: v1.34.3
Kustomize Version: v5.7.1
Server Version: v1.34.3
```

</details>

Also crashes on v1.35.0, and probably older versions.


### Cloud provider

<details>
None: bare metal
</details>


### OS version

<details>

```console
$ cat /etc/os-release
NAME="Fedora Linux"
VERSION="43 (Forty Three)"
RELEASE_TYPE=stable
$ uname -a
Linux fedora 6.18.7-200.fc43.x86_64 #1 SMP PREEMPT_DYNAMIC Fri Jan 23 16:42:34 UTC 2026 x86_64 GNU/Linux
```

</details>


### Install tools

<details>
kubeadm
</details>


### Container runtime (CRI) and version (if applicable)

_No response_

### Related plugins (CNI, CSI, ...) and versions (if applicable)

_No response_

kubernetes/kubernetes#136823.body:
## Why is this needed?

The `Reflector` in `client-go/tools/cache` uses `wait.NewExponentialBackoffManager()` which is deprecated. The deprecation notice recommends using `wait.Backoff{}.DelayWithReset()` instead.

Related issues #117829 and #118638 noted that the deprecation documentation is incomplete - it suggests using `delayFn.Timer()` with `BackoffUntil`, but this doesn't compile because `Timer` doesn't implement `BackoffManager`. 

This issue proposes:
1. Replace `NewExponentialBackoffManager` with `Backoff{}.DelayWithReset()` in the Reflector
2. Use `DelayFunc.Until()` as the loop mechanism (which does work, unlike the documented suggestion)
3. Optionally expose `Backoff` configuration in `ReflectorOptions` for customization

## Benefits
- Removes usage of deprecated API
- Enables context-aware cancellation (BackoffManager doesn't respect context)
- Allows optional customization of backoff strategy when constructing Reflectors

/sig api-machinery
/kind cleanup
/priority backlog
kubernetes/kubernetes#136823.title: client-go: Replace deprecated BackoffManager with DelayFunc in Reflector

kubernetes/kubernetes#136890.body:
### What happened?

When the kubelet creates a `PodCertificateRequest` (KEP-4317, `certificates.k8s.io/v1alpha1`), it sets the owning Pod's `ownerReference.apiVersion` to `"core/v1"` instead of the correct `"v1"`. The Kubernetes garbage collector cannot resolve `"core/v1"` as a valid API group/version for Pods — Pods belong to the legacy core API group, which has no group prefix, so the correct `apiVersion` is simply `"v1"`.

This causes the GC controller in controller-manager to log errors like:

```
error syncing item" err="unable to get REST mapping for core/v1/Pod."
```

When a Pod is deleted, its associated `PodCertificateRequest` objects are never garbage collected.

### What did you expect to happen?

The `ownerReference` on `PodCertificateRequest` objects should use `apiVersion: "v1"` for Pod references, consistent with how all other core-group resources are referenced throughout Kubernetes. The garbage collector should be able to resolve the owner, and when a Pod is deleted, its `PodCertificateRequest` objects should be automatically cleaned up.

### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

1. Enable the `PodCertificateRequest` feature gate (KEP-4317) on the kubelet and kube-apiserver.
2. Deploy a Pod that triggers creation of a `PodCertificateRequest` (e.g., a Pod configured to use pod-identity certificates).
3. Inspect the resulting `PodCertificateRequest` object:
   ```bash
   kubectl get podcertificaterequest -o json | jq '.items[].metadata.ownerReferences'
   ```
4. Observe that `apiVersion` is set to `"core/v1"` instead of `"v1"`.
5. Delete the owning Pod.
6. Observe that the `PodCertificateRequest` is **not** garbage collected.
7. Check the kube-controller-manager logs for GC errors:
   ```
   error syncing item" err="unable to get REST mapping for core/v1/Pod."
   ```

### Anything else we need to know?

**Workaround:** A external signer controller can patch the owner reference from `"core/v1"` to `"v1"` on every `PodCertificateRequest` it reconciles. This restores proper garbage collection behavior.

First bug report, not sure if this should be opened here or commented on https://github.com/kubernetes/enhancements/issues/4317

I am happy to submit the patch for this myself, after I figure out the contrib process. :) 

### Kubernetes version

Affects all versions implementing KEP-4317 (`PodCertificateRequest` / `certificates.k8s.io/v1alpha1` / `certificates.k8s.io/v1beta1`).

### Cloud provider

All

### OS version




### Install tools

_No response_

### Container runtime (CRI) and version (if applicable)




### Related plugins (CNI, CSI, ...) and versions (if applicable)
kubernetes/kubernetes#136890.title: Kubelet sets incorrect ownerReference apiVersion "core/v1" on PodCertificateRequest, breaking garbage collection

kubernetes/kubernetes#137011.body:
### What would you like to be added?

Add an option to the [resourceslice controller](https://github.com/kubernetes/dynamic-resource-allocation/blob/master/resourceslice/resourceslicecontroller.go) (e.g. **`ReconcilePoolWithName`**) so that when it is set (e.g. to the node name):

- **Owner** remains Node (OwnerRef for GC when the node is removed).
- **Do not** set `ResourceSlice.Spec.NodeName` so the scheduler can use `NodeSelector` or `allNodes`.
- **Watch** all ResourceSlices for the driver (no `ResourceSliceSelectorNodeName` in list/watch), but in the informer **only enqueue work for slices whose `Spec.Pool.Name == ReconcilePoolWithName`**. Each node’s controller then reconciles only its own pool (e.g. pool name = node name).

**Backward compatibility:** when the option is empty, keep current behavior (node filter + NodeName when Owner is Node).

**Example 1 - initInformer:** when `poolName` is set, do not add node name to the field selector; in Add/Update/Delete handlers, ignore events where `slice.Spec.Pool.Name != c.poolName`:

```go
if c.owner != nil && c.owner.APIVersion == "v1" && c.owner.Kind == "Node" && c.poolName == "" {
        selector[resourceapi.ResourceSliceSelectorNodeName] = c.owner.Name
}
// In AddFunc / UpdateFunc / DeleteFunc:
if c.poolName != "" && slice.Spec.Pool.Name != c.poolName {
        return
}
```

**Example 2 - syncPool:** when `poolName` is set, do not set NodeName on the slice (keep OwnerRef for GC):

```go
if c.poolName != "" {
        nodeName = ""  // do not set ResourceSlice.Spec.NodeName
}
```

### Why is this needed?

We implemented a DRA USB plugin for **Deckhouse virtualization** with network sharing via USB/IP: USB devices from any node can be attached to pods on any other node ([github.com/deckhouse/virtualization](https://github.com/deckhouse/virtualization), `images/virtualization-dra`). Each node runs usbipd; with USB Gateway, a pod on node B can use a device that is physically on node A.

#### Flow (pod requests USB from another node)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Pod as Pod
    participant DRA as DRA (node with pod)
    participant usbipd as usbipd (node with USB)
    participant USB as USB device

    Pod->>DRA: ResourceClaim (device from another node)
    DRA->>usbipd: 1) Export(host, busID)
    usbipd->>USB: device available for export
    DRA->>usbipd: 2) Attach(host, busID) — vhci connects
    usbipd->>DRA: USB/IP link
    DRA->>DRA: device appears locally (vhci)
    DRA->>Pod: CDI / device into pod
```

We need ResourceSlices **without** `NodeName` (scheduler can place pods on any gateway node) but **with** `NodeSelector` (e.g. `usb-gateway=true`) and **OwnerRef to Node** (GC when the node with the device is removed).

**Current controller behavior:**

1. **If `Owner = Node`** - the controller sets `ResourceSlice.Spec.NodeName`. The API forbids both `NodeName` and `NodeSelector`. So we cannot use `NodeSelector` when the slice is node-owned; scheduling is forced to that single node.

2. **If we leave `NodeName` unset and use `NodeSelector`** - we must not filter list/watch by node. Then every controller instance (one per node) watches all ResourceSlices for the driver and reconciles all pools; they overwrite each other and conflict.

So: **NodeName set ⇒ no NodeSelector**. **NodeSelector without NodeName ⇒ controllers conflict.** There is no way to get “node-owned for GC, cluster-visible via NodeSelector” without this change.

**Validation:** I applied the two changes above locally and verified that the flow works (node-owned slices, no NodeName, NodeSelector for gateway nodes, no conflicts between nodes). We’d like this supported upstream so that “node-owned, cluster-visible” resources (USB/IP or any resource hosted on one node but usable on others) don’t require maintaining a fork.
kubernetes/kubernetes#137011.title: DRA: ResourceSlice controller - per-node reconciliation for network-shared resources (NodeSelector)

kubernetes/kubernetes#137024.body:
### What happened?

I wanted to use a recently introduced feature called "image pull credential verification". It has been described at:

1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/containers/images/#ensureimagepullcredentialverification
2. https://github.com/kubernetes/enhancements/tree/master/keps/sig-node/2535-ensure-secret-pulled-images
3. https://kubernetes.io/blog/2025/05/12/kubernetes-v1-33-ensure-secret-pulled-images-alpha/

I execute scenario with following conditions:

1. registry always requires authentication for the image in question
2. two sample pods are created which use the same image
3. pod definition requests to be scheduled to a specific node and it is always started there
4. the node does not have the image locally initially

The scenario:

1. pod A is started in namespace A without specifying service account or image secrets
2. default service account in ns A has a secret attached for the registry in question
3. kubelet pulls the image successfully
4. pod B is started in namespace B, the default service account has no secrets attached
5. pod B is successfully started but it should fail, this is the problem of this issue

This is what I observe. When the second pod is created an event is generated:

> $ k get events | grep 'already present' | tail -n1
> 42m         Normal    Pulled                 pod/sample-pod   Container image "zot.kube01.pemlab.net:5001/docker-io/rockylinux/rockylinux:10" already present on machine and can be accessed by the pod

This means the verification passed by it should not.

I have a standard configuration of kubelet with imagePullCredentialsVerificationPolicy set to NeverVerifyPreloadedImages. When this setting is changed to AlwaysVerify the attempt fails properly with message about missing credentials.

i did some digging in the source code to find the cause of it. On the second pod the kubelet thinks the image was not pulled by kubelet so it is treated like a preloaded image. After the first pod is created I see this:

```
$ jq . /var/lib/kubelet/image_manager/pulled/sha256-dd81f502dc5e7f8b60445496abe590916d606411d7c474a2e18e4a42e4e82151
{
  "kind": "ImagePulledRecord",
  "apiVersion": "kubelet.config.k8s.io/v1beta1",
  "lastUpdatedTime": "2026-02-14T09:41:58Z",
  "imageRef": "zot.kube01.pemlab.net:5001/docker-io/rockylinux/rockylinux@sha256:b86cb4305db4c29abb1e3a1257fefccc184b5e4a94378e02844b340845ef9460",
  "credentialMapping": {
    "zot.kube01.pemlab.net:5001/docker-io/rockylinux/rockylinux": {
      "kubernetesSecrets": [
        {
          "uid": "eeb25318-beff-46d0-bb24-f0c871a11e25",
          "namespace": "kube-system",
          "name": "zot",
          "credentialHash": "0bd56ac49132f1c4ffbf88affd024c6888e40d35fb6c9e10fef0e0b19a09fb06"
        }
      ]
    }
  }
}
```

The ns A is the kube-system in this case. Note the imageRef as it will be used below. It has a proper sha for rockylinux:10 for linux/amd64 platform at the time this issue is created. zot registry is being used and the /docker-io/** path needs authentication.

The message is generated from https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/release-1.35/pkg/kubelet/images/image_manager.go#L257. The function receives argument of requestedImage with value "zot.kube01.pemlab.net:5001/docker-io/rockylinux/rockylinux:10". In imagePullPrecheck it uses imageService to resolve the image to an imageRef and it receives a value of "812a83d5aec123cbd962ba7cd1e22266db0fe2b3a0b49cb4e9aa521d13ad214f". It matches:
```
$ crictl inspecti zot.kube01.pemlab.net:5001/docker-io/rockylinux/rockylinux:10 | yq .status.id
812a83d5aec123cbd962ba7cd1e22266db0fe2b3a0b49cb4e9aa521d13ad214f
```

The call in https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/release-1.35/pkg/kubelet/images/image_manager.go#L253 uses the requestedImage and imageRef to decide if the image needs to be pulled or not. The MustAttemptImagePull contains a lot of conditionals and yet it doesn't have any debug logging. I had to modify the source code and add print statements everywhere to understand what is going on. The https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/release-1.35/pkg/kubelet/images/pullmanager/image_pull_manager.go#L211 tries to find by imageRef and returns nil, false, nil. There are no intents at this time so imagePulledByKubelet stays with false value. This in turn is processed by the policy and we have the decision.

Now I wondered why the find failed. The records are stored in memory. The lookup in https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/release-1.35/pkg/kubelet/images/pullmanager/mem_pullrecords.go#L197 uses the imageRef into a map. The key isn't there. So I made an investigation when keys are added and the added key was: 

```
zot.kube01.pemlab.net:5001/docker-io/rockylinux/rockylinux@sha256:b86cb4305db4c29abb1e3a1257fefccc184b5e4a94378e02844b340845ef9460
```

and this is what is read from /var/lib/kubelet/image_manager/pulled.

I tested this with cri-o and containerd. There is no difference. I wonder how this verification can ever work if there is always mismatch is inserted and lookedup keys ?

I have tested this with the directory clean and a fresh start of kubelet and the directory populated with files and the result is the same. The cache is populated with very long keys yet lookups are done with short imageID as reported by crictl.

### What did you expect to happen?

I expect the kubelet to verify the credentials again but it doesn't do it.

### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

Scenario in the issue description.

### Anything else we need to know?

_No response_

### Kubernetes version

```
$ kubectl version
Client Version: v1.35.0
Kustomize Version: v5.7.1
Server Version: v1.35.0
```


### Cloud provider

On premise.

### OS version

Linux/amd64.

### Install tools

kubernetes/kubernetes#137058.body:
With #136799, we introduced the [doRequestWithPortForward](https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/master/test/e2e/framework/autoscaling/autoscaling_utils.go#L207)  helper, which manually sets up port-forwarding.

Instead of maintaining a custom port-forward implementation, we should replace this logic by directly invoking kubectl’s existing implementation via [PortForwardOptions.RunPortForwardContext](https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/master/staging/src/k8s.io/kubectl/pkg/cmd/portforward/portforward.go#L421).

Ref: https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/136799#discussion_r2811866607 
/triage accepted 
/good-first-issue 
/sig autoscaling 
/kind cleanup
kubernetes/kubernetes#137058.title: Cleanup: Replace manual port-forward implementation with kubectl PortForwardOptions

kubernetes/kubernetes#137074.body:
In the HPA e2e tests, the NewDynamicResourceConsumer utility function is responsible for creating several Kubernetes objects. However, these objects are currently created using deprecated/legacy API versions:
https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/2fb84cfed3e0da83eec85dcc97d14db9b06915c9/test/e2e/framework/autoscaling/autoscaling_utils.go#L90-L98

We should update this implementation to use the latest stable API versions available. Additionally, the current usage of `ReplicationController` should be replaced with `ReplicaSet`.

/triage accepted
/good-first-issue
/sig autoscaling
/kind cleanup
kubernetes/kubernetes#137074.title: Update HPA e2e DynamicResourceConsumer to use current APIs

kubernetes/kubernetes#137093.body:
### What happened?

The container_swap_usage_bytes metric exposed by Kubelet's resource metrics API (/metrics/resource) consistently reports 0, even when containers are actively using swap.

```
container_swap_usage_bytes{container="event-exporter",namespace="kube-system",pod="event-exporter-gke-54578fb7bb-cdmd4"} 0 1771366453461
```

Stats summary shows the correct usage:

```
{
   "podRef": {
    "name": "event-exporter-gke-54578fb7bb-cdmd4",
    "namespace": "kube-system",
    "uid": "43bbf834-faf8-4f46-b016-b468c283382a"
   },
   "startTime": "2026-02-17T19:57:38Z",
   "containers": [
    {
     "name": "event-exporter",
     "swap": {
      "time": "2026-02-17T22:15:35Z",
      "swapAvailableBytes": 133222400,
      "swapUsageBytes": 1024000
       }
     }
  ]
}
```

The pod_swap_usage_bytes metric also reports the correct value. Additionally, the Kubelet summary API (/stats/summary) correctly populates the swap usage for both pods and containers.

```
pod_swap_usage_bytes{namespace="kube-system",pod="event-exporter-gke-54578fb7bb-cdmd4"} 1.024e+06 1771366463287
```

### What did you expect to happen?

The container_swap_usage_bytes metric in /metrics/resource should report the actual swap usage of the container, consistent with the data available in cAdvisor and the summary API.

### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

1. Enable swap on a Linux node and configure Kubelet to support it.
2. Deploy a pod that actively consumes swap memory.
3. Query the Kubelet resource metrics endpoint: /metrics/resource
4. Observe that container_swap_usage_bytes is 0 for the container.
5. Verify that /stats/summary shows a non-zero value for the same container's swap usage.

### Anything else we need to know?

_No response_

### Kubernetes version

$ kubectl version

Kustomize Version: v5.5.0

kubernetes/kubernetes#137109.body:
### What would you like to be added? I propose implementing object interning (deduplication) techniques in the Kubernetes API Server and control plane components to significantly reduce memory usage. Specifically, this proposal suggests: * **String Interning:** Modifying protobuf deserialization to intern strings during decoding. [PoC](https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/commit/c8ff1377dcebe18effe75166507bf624afbe008e) * **ManagedFields Deduplication:** Implementing custom deserialization for FieldsV1 (Server-Side Apply metadata) to reuse existing objects. [PoC](https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/commit/627898045327206f37b347c65ba84d0fdb69b361) * **JSON Optimization:** Evaluating a migration to json/v2 for built-in interning support. Work related to https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/134375 ### Why is this needed? As Kubernetes adoption grows, particularly with the rise of AI/ML workloads, cluster scales are pushing beyond traditional limits. * **High Replication & Redundancy:** We are increasingly seeing Jobs, Deployments, and StatefulSets scaling to 10,000+ replicas. In these highly replicated workloads, the PodSpec is largely identical across replicas, leading to massive redundancy in memory. Etcd already started getting feature request to handle it better [etcd-io/etcd#21305](https://github.com/etcd-io/etcd/issues/21305), * **Memory Bottlenecks:** The API server consumes approximately 10x the raw resource size in memory to maintaining latest state and history in watch cache and latest state in informer. With the increase in replication and resource complexity, memory overhead becomes the primary limiting factor for horizontal scaling. * **ManagedFields Overhead:** By design, Server-Side Apply (SSA) metadata accounts for >50% of the stored resource size[KEP-555](https://github.com/kubernetes/enhancements/tree/master/keps/sig-api-machinery/555-server-side-apply#scalability). Since this data is highly repetitive, it is a prime candidate for deduplication. ### Alternatives Considered * **Reflect-based interning:** Using reflection to intern strings after deserialization was rejected because it incurred a 2.5x CPU overhead and increased allocations by 25%. * **Full PodSpec interning:** Caching and reusing the entire PodSpec struct was explored but deemed too risky. Initial implementations caused API server crashes due to concurrent map writes, and while deep-copying mitigated this, the approach remains fragile due to hidden mutations (e.g., defaulting, admission). * **Dropping unused Informer fields:** We considered dropping unused fields from the API Server's internal informers. However, this provided only marginal benefits (~5% memory reduction) restricted to the API server, without addressing the broader KCP memory footprint. ### Analysis & Results We evaluated these techniques using a synthetic benchmark with a realistic workload. 1. **Optimistic microbenchmark Results (per object)** 13 pod manifests decoded 10,000 times. | Technique | Decode time [µs] | KBs retained per object | KBs allocated per object | Allocations per object | | ---------------------------------------- | ---------------- | ----------------------- | ------------------------ | ---------------------- | | Base | 29.33 | 24.15 | 29.13 | 346.0 | | ReflectStrings | 87.63 (+198.8%) | 19.07 (-21.0%) | 34.34 (+17.9%) | 433.0 (+25.1%) | | DecodeString | 35.01 (+19.3%) | 19.07 (-21.1%) | 24.17 (-17.0%) | 154.0 (-55.5%) | | ManagedFields | 27.19 (~) | 19.10 (-20.9%) | 24.20 (-16.9%) | 343.0 (-0.9%) | | DecodeString
ManagedFields | 33.51 (~) | 14.02 (-42.0%) | 19.24 (-34.0%) | 151.0 (-56.4%) | | PodSpec | 12.29 (-58.1%) | 13.29 (-45.0%) | 14.10 (-51.6%) | 115.0 (-66.8%) | | DecodeString
ManagedFields
PodSpec | 11.11 (-62.1%) | 6.98 (-71.1%) | 7.93 (-72.8%) | 56.0 (-83.8%) | 2. **Pessimistic microbenchmark Results (per object)** 13 unique pod manifests decoded 10 times (expected times a healthy pod is written in deployment cycle) | Technique | Decode time [µs] | KBs retained per object | KBs allocated per object | Allocations per object | | ---------------------------------------- | ---------------- | ----------------------- | ------------------------ | ---------------------- | | Base | 45.60 | 24.15 | 29.13 | 346.0 | | ReflectStrings | 148.65 (+226.0%) | 19.84 (-17.9%) | 37.75 (+29.6%) | 541.0 (+56.4%) | | DecodeString | 75.57 (+65.7%) | 19.56 (-19.0%) | 26.35 (-9.5%) | 222.0 (-35.8%) | | ManagedFields | 41.25 (-9.5%) | 19.96 (-17.4%) | 25.89 (-11.1%) | 344.0 (-0.6%) | | DecodeString
ManagedFields | 74.61 (+63.6%) | 15.38 (-36.3%) | 23.13 (-20.6%) | 220.0 (-36.4%) | | PodSpec | 24.58 (-46.1%) | 15.02 (-37.8%) | 17.24 (-40.8%) | 153.0 (-55.8%) | | DecodeString
ManagedFields
PodSpec | 34.21 (-25.0%) | 8.44 (-65.1%) | 11.83 (-59.4%) | 107.0 (-69.1%) | 3. **Component Memory Usage** Cluster state with 50k replicated pods resulting in around 1GB of resource size data stored in etcd. | Technique | API-server Memory | KCM Memory | Scheduler Memory | KCP memory SUM | | -------------------------------------- | ----------------- | ----------------- | ----------------- | ------------------ | | Base | 9.98 GB | 3.05 GB | 5.14 GB | 18.18 GB | | ReflectStrings | 8.64 GB (-13.42%) | 2.64 GB (-13.41%) | 4.38 GB (-14.68%) | 15.67 GB (-13.77%) | | DecodeString | 8.22 GB (-17.71%) | 2.78 GB (-8.90%) | 4.35 GB (-15.41%) | 15.34 GB (-15.58%) | | ManagedFields | 8.52 GB (-14.67%) | 3.15 GB (~) | 3.90 GB (-24.03%) | 15.58 GB (-14.30%) | | DecodeString
ManagedFields | 7.40 GB (-25.86%) | 2.63 GB (-13.92%) | 3.43 GB (-33.31%) | 13.46 GB (-25.96%) | | PodSpec | 7.64 GB (-23.48%) | 3.28 GB (~) | 4.69 GB (~) | 15.60 GB (-14.16%) | | DecodeString
ManagedFields
PodSpec | 5.69 GB (-43.01%) | 2.54 GB (-16.81%) | 3.13 GB (-39.18%) | 11.36 GB (-37.52%) | | DropInformerFields | 9.38 GB (-6.01%) | 3.05 GB (~) | 5.25 GB (~) | 17.68 GB (-2.71%) | **Conclusion:** Implementing string and ManagedFields interning yields a ~25% reduction in total KCP memory usage for highly replicated workloads. This memory reduction is essential to support the increased resource sizes and replication counts required by modern workloads. /cc @jpbetz @liggitt @mborsz @wojtek-t @deads2k
kubernetes/kubernetes#137109.title: Interning for API Server and controller memory reduction

kubernetes/kubernetes#137135.body:
### What happened?

When a container's `restartPolicyRules[].exitCodes.values` length exceeds 255 elements, the validation error message says:

```bash
containers[0].restartPolicyRules[0].exitCodes.values: Too long: may not be more than 255 bytes
```

The validation uses field.TooLong (byte length checks) instead of field.TooMany (slice length checks).

### What did you expect to happen?

The field is []int32, and the length limit is at most 255 elements, not 255 bytes.
If I am not wrong, I think the correct error message should be:
```bash
containers[0].restartPolicyRules[0].exitCodes.values: Too many: 256: must have at most 255 items
```

### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/master/pkg/apis/core/validation/validation.go#L3716

### Anything else we need to know?

_No response_

kubernetes/kubernetes#137269.body:
While reviewing https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/137028/files, I noticed that any addition of any group/version results in changes to the openapi v3 published for all other group versions.

This happens because we cross-register generic metav1 types like DeleteOptions and WatchEvent into all API group/versions, and then publish all registered group/versions for those types into openapiv3 anywhere they are listed.

Consider limiting the published group/versions for things like DeleteOptions and WatchEvent to the meta.k8s.io/v1 and local group/version.

/sig api-machinery
/kind cleanup
/cc @jpbetz
kubernetes/kubernetes#137269.title: Investigate cleaning up cross-group publishing of Options types in openapi v3

kubernetes/kubernetes#137331.body:
### What happened?

When creating a pod with a projected `serviceAccountToken` volume and all containers have the same `runAsUser`, kubelet configure the file owner UID of the token file to the the `runAsUser`.

However, the existing implementation only sets the file owner of file in the timestamped directory. The link file is still owned by root. This is because `pkg/volume/util/atomic_writer_linux.go#chown` makes use of `os.Chown()`, which applies changes to the link's target instead of the link file itself.

We can use `os.Lchown()` to configure owner UID of the link file properly.

```
$ ls -lA
total 0
drwxr-xr-x    2 root     root            60 Mar  1 19:17 ..2026_03_01_19_17_08.1191223127
lrwxrwxrwx    1 root     root            32 Mar  1 19:17 ..data -> ..2026_03_01_19_17_08.1191223127
lrwxrwxrwx    1 root     root            12 Mar  1 19:17 token -> ..data/token

$ ls -lA ..2026_03_01_19_17_08.1191223127/
total 4
-rw-------    1 1000     root          1089 Mar  1 19:17 token

$ ls -lA ..data/
total 4
-rw-------    1 1000     root          1089 Mar  1 19:17 token
```

### What did you expect to happen?

Both the file in the timestamped directory and the link file should be owned by `runAsUser`.

### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

```
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: token-test
spec:
  securityContext:
    runAsUser: 1000
  containers:
  - image: busybox:1.28
    name: token-test
    command: ["tail", "-f", "/dev/null"]
    volumeMounts:
    - name: token
      mountPath: /mnt/token
  volumes:
  - name: token
    projected:
      sources:
        - serviceAccountToken:
            audience: api
            expirationSeconds: 3600
            path: token
```

### Anything else we need to know?

_No response_

### Kubernetes version

<details>
Server Version: v1.36.0-alpha.1.307+dbe44e35849f2e-dirty

I compiled from master.
</details>


### Cloud provider

Not applicable. I'm running master locally.

### OS version

_No response_

### Install tools

_No response_

### Container runtime (CRI) and version (if applicable)

_No response_

### Related plugins (CNI, CSI, ...) and versions (if applicable)

_No response_
kubernetes/kubernetes#137331.title: Link file ownership of projected serviceAccountToken is not set properly

`logger="TestDRA/all/ControllerManagerMetrics leaked goroutine.UnhandledError"` shows that the "TestDRA/all/ControllerManagerMetrics" kept something running beyond the end of the test, probably the claim controller. "UnhandledError" gets added by `runtime.HandleError`.

`item="claim:testdra-all-shareresourceclaimsequentially-9q88d/external-claim"` shows that one test is operating on claims from another - not good!

The panic in `dynamicresources.go:1247` is probably the allocation result:

https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/b9e0f9d10ae94665d6fc1817f7310672a905ffd0/pkg/scheduler/framework/plugins/dynamicresources/dynamicresources.go#L1247

I think what happens is that the claims gets deallocated while a new pod is about to be added to the `ReservedFor`. `PreBind` should check for that, but doesn't.

I'm still not sure why the controller deallocates, though. That part is still a bit odd.

### Anything else we need to know?

_No response_

### Relevant SIG(s)

/sig scheduling
/wg device-management
/cc @nojnhuh @mortent 
/assign

kubernetes/kubernetes#137368.body:
### What happened?

When validating container restart policy rules with an invalid action name, the error message always shows only `["Restart"]` as supported actions, even when the `RestartAllContainersOnContainerExits` feature gate is enabled.


### What did you expect to happen?

When a user specifies an invalid action value **and** the `RestartAllContainersOnContainerExits` feature gate is enabled, the error message should display ["Restart", "RestartAllContainers"] as supported values

```bash
# expected message
The Pod "***" is invalid: spec.containers[0].restartPolicyRules[0].action: Unsupported value: "invalid": supported values: "Restart", "RestartAllContainers"

# current message
The Pod "***" is invalid: spec.containers[0].restartPolicyRules[0].action: Unsupported value: "invalid": supported values: "Restart"
```

### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

1. Enable the `RestartAllContainersOnContainerExits` feature gate
2. Create a Pod with an invalid container restart policy action name
3. Observe the validation error message

### Anything else we need to know?

_No response_

kubernetes/kubernetes#137401.body:
### What happened?

While working on a dual-stack issue on `kube-proxy` (#114702 #137370), I discovered another issue.

If the IPv6 support is turned off on a node (for example, with sysctl), kube-proxy fails to correctly detect that the node is single‑stack, and logs the following error when a dual-service is deployed:

```
I0304 09:29:42.889348       1 server.go:265] "kube-proxy running in dual-stack mode" primary ipFamily="IPv4"

$ sudo sysctl -a | grep  net.ipv6.conf.all.disable_ipv6
net.ipv6.conf.all.disable_ipv6 = 1
```
If dual-stack mode is enabled incorrectly, the following error occurs:

```
E0304 09:11:04.154332       1 proxier.go:1777] "Failed to bind service address to dummy device" err="error bind address: fd00:1234::f:d72 to interface: kube-ipvs0, err: permission denied" ipFamily="IPv6" serviceName="default/nginx-dual"
E0304 09:11:04.154358       1 proxier.go:1066] "Failed to sync service" err="error bind address: fd00:1234::f:d72 to interface: kube-ipvs0, err: permission denied" ipFamily="IPv6" servicePortName="default/nginx-dual" virtualServer="[fd00:1234::f:d72]:8080/TCP"

```

### What did you expect to happen?

Dual-stack support should not be enabled, and the following message should be logged instead:

`I0304 11:24:11.226354       1 server.go:267] "kube-proxy running in single-stack mode" ipFamily="IPv4"`

The problem is on these lines: https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/bd3c97fbd8d79741ba71f85a1eb342c01266e2b9/cmd/kube-proxy/app/server_linux.go#L107-L112 -- they do not take into account whether IPv6 support has been disabled. This option has existed since Linux kernel v2.6.27.

### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

* Deploy a multi-node, dual-stack Kubernetes cluster.
* Disable IPv6 on one node: `sudo sysctl -w net.ipv6.conf.all.disable_ipv6=1`
* Deploy a Service that requests both address families.
* Observe how `kube-proxy` logs the error.

### Anything else we need to know?

_No response_

### Kubernetes version

<details>

```console
$ kubectl version
Client Version: v1.34.5
Kustomize Version: v5.7.1
Server Version: v1.34.0
```

</details>

kubernetes/kubernetes#137406.body:
During the review of https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/136996 we found that both integer and boolean payload arguments are parsed sloppily and that we should be parsing them canonically such that when converting back to a string the input string and resulting string match.

A utility function for this has been added, but still needs to be updated to be used for all tags that accept an integer payload value.

A utility function does not exist for parsing boolean values and should be added. Once added all tags that parse boolean payload values need to be updated to use this new utility function.

Spun out from:

> Can we please file a bug to do the same for Bool?

_Originally posted by @thockin in https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/136996#discussion_r2861913728_
kubernetes/kubernetes#137406.title: [Declarative Validation] Canonically parse integer and boolean payload values.

kubernetes/kubernetes#137409.body:
### What happened?

I decided to open this issue after investigating https://github.com/stackabletech/trino-operator/issues/854.

The high level description of the problem is that the stateful set controller doesn't update the pods according to the latest STS revision and the pods are left crashlooping **if the first revision is faulty** and can never become available. This doesn't happen if the `MaxUnavailableStatefulSet` feature is disabled.

Unfortunately I cannot create a much simpler example, but I attach the relevant controller log messages below.

Based on these logs you can see that:

1. The Stackable Operator for Trino (`trino-op` from now on) creates two `StatefulSet` objects with a single replica. They both expose the same problem, so for simplicity, I will use only one of them from now on (`trino-coordinator-default`).
2. The initial revision of this sts is `trino-coordinator-default-5f5598d4c8`. This first STS  revision has a faulty command args  and can never become available.
3. The `trino-op` updates the sts rapidly to the revision `trino-coordinator-default-787556ff59`. Now the STS command args are correct and the pods should be able to become ready.
4. The pod created initially is never updated so it never becomes ready.

To summarize my understanding, the sts revision is never updated if the pod never becomes ready, and the pod doesn't become ready because it has an old revision.

In the logs you see that `currentRevision` is never the same as `updateRevision`:

```
I0304 15: 25:06.514308       1 stateful_set_control.go: 146]"StatefulSet revisions" statefulSet = "kuttl-test-distinct-turtle/trino-coordinator-default" currentRevision = "trino-coordinator-default-5f5598d4c8" updateRevision = "trino-coordinator-default-787556ff59"
```

### What did you expect to happen?

The pod should become ready according to the `updateRevision` of the sts.
Again, this was indeed the case until `MaxUnavailableStatefulSet` feature gate was enabled.

### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

I can reproduce this bug using the `trino-op` but unfortunately I don't have a minimal example.

### Anything else we need to know?

Attached are the relevant kube controller logs.

[kobe-controller-logs.txt](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/25745670/kobe-controller-logs.txt)

### Kubernetes version

<details>

```console
$ kubectl version
Client Version: v1.34.0
Kustomize Version: v5.7.1
Server Version: v1.35.1
```

</details>

kubernetes/kubernetes#137437.body:
### Which jobs are flaking?

ci-kubernetes-e2e-autoscaling-hpa-cpu

### Which tests are flaking?

> Kubernetes e2e suite: [It] [sig-autoscaling] [Feature:HPA] [Slow] [FeatureGate:HPAConfigurableTolerance] [Beta] Horizontal pod autoscaling (configurable tolerance) with small scale-up, large scale-down tolerances should scale up but should not scale down 

### Since when has it been flaking?

Since the release of HPAConfigurableTolerance

### Testgrid link

https://testgrid.k8s.io/sig-autoscaling-hpa#gce-cos-autoscaling-hpa-cpu

### Reason for failure (if possible)

See noted in https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/137168

The current way to add load to pods doesn't guarantee that the load is equally spread. In some cases the load isn't spread evenly enough, and since the pods have CPU limits, we can't generate enough load to cause the HPA to scale.

From the linked PR:
> I think the long term solution will be to use a Headless Service, and modify the [resource-consumer-controller](https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/9c607c36573d90140b878bb63789c7ed965ca585/test/images/agnhost/resource-consumer-controller/controller.go) to do the round-robining across each endpoint of that Service, giving us more predicable control of the CPU usage of each pod.

### Anything else we need to know?

_No response_

### Relevant SIG(s)

/sig autoscaling
kubernetes/kubernetes#137437.title: Kubernetes e2e suite: [It] [sig-autoscaling] [Feature:HPA] [Slow] [FeatureGate:HPAConfigurableTolerance] [Beta] Horizontal pod autoscaling (configurable tolerance) with small scale-up, large scale-down tolerances should scale up but should not scale down

kubernetes/kubernetes#137441.body:
The `PodRequestsAndLimits` function in `staging/src/k8s.io/kubectl/pkg/util/resource/resource.go` is deprecated in favor of `k8s.io/component-helpers/resource.PodRequests` and `PodLimits`, which also support pod-level resources. There are no remaining consumers in kubectl. This function and its unexported helpers (`podRequests`, `podLimits`) should be removed.

Follow-up from #137394.

/kind cleanup
/sig cli
/cc @soltysh
kubernetes/kubernetes#137441.title: Remove deprecated PodRequestsAndLimits from kubectl/pkg/util/resource

kubernetes/kubernetes#137445.body:
## Background

[PR #137408](https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/137408) disabled the hard-fail on the deadcode-elimination check to unblock the Go 1.26 update. This was necessary because a change in `reflect` in Go 1.26 trips up deadcode elimination in several libraries used by Kubernetes. Additional issues remain in upstream dependencies. Once the upstream libraries have released fixes, re-enable the hard-fail on the deadcode-elimination check.

xref:
- PR that disabled the check: https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/137408
- Go 1.26 upstream issue: https://github.com/golang/go/issues/77235#issuecomment-3999314652
- golang/protobuf issue: https://github.com/golang/protobuf/issues/1704
- kube-openapi fix: https://github.com/kubernetes/kube-openapi/pull/574
kubernetes/kubernetes#137445.title: Track dependency updates and restore deadcode-elimination hard-fail for Go 1.26

### Which jobs are failing?

https://testgrid.k8s.io/sig-cluster-lifecycle-kubeadm#kubeadm-kinder-external-ca-latest
https://testgrid.k8s.io/sig-cluster-lifecycle-kubeadm#kubeadm-kinder-kubelet-1-34-on-latest

and others:
https://testgrid.k8s.io/sig-cluster-lifecycle-kubeadm

### Which tests are failing?

cluster-info task

### Since when has it been failing?

06 march 2026

### Testgrid link

_No response_

### Reason for failure (if possible)

kinder calls etcdctl --version` and makes sure it's parseable. but a recent change in the image results in the version being just `etcd`

```
etcd-kinder-xony-control-plane-1 << registry.k8s.io/etcd:3.6.8-0, 
etcd-kinder-xony-control-plane-2 << registry.k8s.io/etcd:3.6.8-0, 
etcd-kinder-xony-control-plane-3 << registry.k8s.io/etcd:3.6.8-0, 
...
kinder-xony-control-plane-1:$ kubectl --request-timeout=2 --kubeconfig=/etc/kubernetes/admin.conf exec -n=kube-system etcd-kinder-xony-control-plane-1 -- etcd --version
time="10:50:47" level=debug msg="Running: docker exec kinder-xony-control-plane-1 kubectl --request-timeout=2 --kubeconfig=/etc/kubernetes/admin.conf exec -n=kube-system etcd-kinder-xony-control-plane-1 -- etcd --version"

kinder-xony-control-plane-1:$ Using etcdctl version: etcd

Error: failed to exec action cluster-info: cannot parse etcd version: could not parse "etcd" as version
 exit status 1
```

~the etcd binary should return it's version info properly.~

EDIT: root cause explained here:
https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/137532#issuecomment-4016281181


### Anything else we need to know?

_No response_

### Relevant SIG(s)

/sig cluster-lifecycle etcd

cc @pacoxu @ahrtr

kubernetes/kubernetes#137595.body:
### What would you like to be added?

Go 1.26 allows `new(x)` in place of `ptr.To(x)`. The `modernize` linter surfaces this as a hint (`newexpr`). We should hint at changes like this in master only when at least one older stable branch is also on Go 1.26.

In practice this issue can be resolved by reverting https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/137596.

### Why is this needed?

Avoid pushing developers towards features which will cause problems when the new code needs to be backported.
kubernetes/kubernetes#137595.title: golangci-lint: re-enable `newexpr` hint in modernize linter

kubernetes/kubernetes#137617.body:
### What happened?

When two Pods are created in a gang where both Pods share the same ResourceClaim, the scheduler falls into an endless loop trying to schedule the Pods and never succeeds.

```
Events:
  Type     Reason            Age                          From               Message
  ----     ------            ----                         ----               -------
  Warning  FailedScheduling  2m21s (x2292168 over 7m21s)  default-scheduler  0/2 nodes are available: resource claim default/single-gpu is in the process of being allocated. no new claims to deallocate, preemption: 0/2 nodes are available: 2 Preemption is not helpful for scheduling.
```

### What did you expect to happen?

Both Pods schedule with the shared ResourceClaim.

### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

Create a kind cluster:
```
kind build node-image --image kindest/node:latest
kind create cluster --image kindest/node:latest --config kind.yaml
```
<details>
<summary>kind.yaml</summary>

```yaml
kind: Cluster
apiVersion: kind.x-k8s.io/v1alpha4
nodes:
- role: control-plane
- role: worker
kubeadmConfigPatches:
  - |
    kind: ClusterConfiguration
    apiVersion: kubeadm.k8s.io/v1beta3
    apiServer:
        extraArgs:
          runtime-config: "scheduling.k8s.io/v1alpha2=true"
featureGates:
  GenericWorkload: true
  GangScheduling: true
```

</details>

Install a DRA driver:
```
helm upgrade --install dra-example-driver oci://registry.k8s.io/dra-example-driver/charts/dra-example-driver -n dra-example-driver --create-namespace
```

Create a ResourceClaim, PodGroup, and Pods in the PodGroup sharing the claim:
<details>
<summary>workload.yaml</summary>

```yaml
apiVersion: resource.k8s.io/v1
kind: ResourceClaim
metadata:
  name: single-gpu
spec:
  devices:
    requests:
    - name: gpu
      exactly:
        deviceClassName: gpu.example.com
---
apiVersion: scheduling.k8s.io/v1alpha2
kind: PodGroup
metadata:
  name: podgroup
spec:
  schedulingPolicy:
    gang:
      minCount: 2
---
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: my-app
spec:
  replicas: 2
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: my-app
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: my-app
    spec:
      resourceClaims:
        - name: claim
          resourceClaimName: single-gpu
      containers:
        - name: ctr
          image: ubuntu:22.04
          command: ["bash", "-c"]
          args: ["export; trap 'exit 0' TERM; sleep 9999 & wait"]
          resources:
            claims:
              - name: claim
      schedulingGroup:
        podGroupName: podgroup
```
</details>

### Anything else we need to know?

Both Pods will schedule together if either of the following changes are made:
- Set the PodGroup's `spec.schedulingPolicy` to `basic: {}`
- Create separate ResourceClaims for each Pod instead of sharing (ResourceClaimTemplate).

### Kubernetes version

<details>

```console
$ kubectl version
Client Version: v1.35.1
Kustomize Version: v5.7.1
Server Version: v1.36.0-alpha.2.435+0009958799e560
```

</details>


### Cloud provider

<details>
Local kind cluster
</details>


### OS version

Kind node:
<details>

```console
$ cat /etc/os-release
PRETTY_NAME="Debian GNU/Linux 12 (bookworm)"
NAME="Debian GNU/Linux"
VERSION_ID="12"
VERSION="12 (bookworm)"
VERSION_CODENAME=bookworm
ID=debian
HOME_URL="https://www.debian.org/"
SUPPORT_URL="https://www.debian.org/support"
BUG_REPORT_URL="https://bugs.debian.org/"
$ uname -a
Linux kind-control-plane 6.17.0-1008-azure #8~24.04.1-Ubuntu SMP Mon Jan 26 18:35:40 UTC 2026 x86_64 GNU/Linux
```

</details>


### Install tools

<details>

```
% kind version
kind v0.31.0 go1.26.1 linux/amd64
```
</details>


### Container runtime (CRI) and version (if applicable)

<details>

</details>


### Related plugins (CNI, CSI, ...) and versions (if applicable)

<details>
DRA Example Driver v0.2.1
</details>

kubernetes/kubernetes#137670.body:
https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/137170#pullrequestreview-3924450429 points out a gap `GetResetFields()` usage in APIs and suggests we have a systemic test coverage gap, and likely other cases in the API where `GetResetFields()` is not set correctly.

We need to find a way to test all APIs for correct field resetting.  The field resetting should match the update field clearing for subresources.

Gaps found in testing should be fixed.  The goal is to fix all existing gaps and establish testing that covers all current and future APIs.
kubernetes/kubernetes#137670.title: Add comprehensive API test coverage for field wiping and resetting

[3/12/2026, 12:37:58 PM ci-cloud-provider-aws-e2e-kubetest2](https://prow.k8s.io/view/gs/kubernetes-ci-logs/logs/ci-cloud-provider-aws-e2e-kubetest2/2032133621090881536)


/kind failing-test
<!-- If this is a flake, please add: /kind flake -->

#### Analysis

**AI Analysis**

  ---
  Summary

  46 DRA tests failed, 23 passed. Every single failure has the same root cause:

  Root Cause: dra-test-driver ReplicaSet never becomes available

  All 46 test failures occur in BeforeEach at test/e2e/dra/utils/deploy.go:575 with one of two errors:
  1. replicaset "dra-test-driver" never had desired number of .status.availableReplicas (most failures)
  2. client rate limiter Wait returned an error: rate: Wait(n=1) would exceed context deadline (a few failures)

  The test driver pods can't start because the worker kubelets are crash-looping.

  Kubelet Errors (all 3 workers identical)

  Critical — causes kubelet crash-loop (1,315 restarts on kind-worker alone):

    │ CPU manager checkpoint corrupted — "could not restore state from checkpoint: checkpoint is corrupted, please drain this node and delete the CPU manager checkpoint file /var/lib/kubelet/cpu_manager_state before restarting Kubelet" │ cpu_manager.go:231 │


https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/134768#discussion_r2935673466

In the last two days the only change that seems to change anything around cpu_manager_state was the above. 

cc @KevinTMtz @pohly 

/sig node

kubernetes/kubernetes#137823.body:
It looks like we never added v1.35.0 testdata to https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/tree/master/staging/src/k8s.io/api/testdata

Before releasing 1.36, we need to ensure compatibility with 1.35 data by cloning testdata/HEAD at v1.35.0 to a new directory and ensuring nothing merged to 1.36 impacted round-tripping those types losslessly

/milestone v1.36
/label release-blocker

kubernetes/kubernetes#137858.body:
### What happened?

After I create a pod using a Kubernetes deployment in an empty Kind cluster, I see an in-place update event that seems unnecessary:

```bash
kubectl get events --watch -A

default              0s          Normal    ResizeCompleted           pod/hamster-deployment-57445548f6-r27rn          Pod resize completed: {"containers":[{"name":"hamster1","resources":{"requests":{"cpu":"10m","memory":"10Mi"}}},{"name":"hamster2","resources":{}},{"name":"hamster3","resources":{}}],"generation":1}
```

### What did you expect to happen?

I don't expect to see an in-place update after deploying the pod to the cluster.

### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

```bash
KIND_VERSION="kindest/node:v1.35.0@sha256:452d707d4862f52530247495d180205e029056831160e22870e37e3f6c1ac31f"

kind create cluster --image=${KIND_VERSION} --config <(cat <<EOF
kind: Cluster
apiVersion: kind.x-k8s.io/v1alpha4
featureGates:
  "InPlacePodLevelResourcesVerticalScaling": true
  "NodeDeclaredFeatures": true
EOF
)
```

```yaml
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  labels:
    app: hamster
  name: hamster-deployment
  namespace: default
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: hamster
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: hamster
    spec:
      resources:
        requests:
          cpu: 100m
          memory: 100Mi
      containers:
      - name: hamster1
        image: registry.k8s.io/pause:3.1
        resources:
          requests:
            cpu: 10m
            memory: 10Mi
      - image: registry.k8s.io/pause:3.1
        name: hamster2
      - image: registry.k8s.io/pause:3.1
        name: hamster3
EOF
```

### Anything else we need to know?

* I don't have anything like Vertical Pod Autoscaler that should trigger in-place updates at any level (container or pod).
* related slack thread: https://kubernetes.slack.com/archives/C06FSK01BGU/p1773818099854269

### Kubernetes version

<details>

```console
$ kubectl version
Client Version: v1.35.1
Kustomize Version: v5.7.1
Server Version: v1.35.0
```

</details>


### Cloud provider

<details>
kind cluster
</details>


### OS version

<details>

```console
# On Linux:
$ cat /etc/os-release
NAME="Manjaro Linux"
PRETTY_NAME="Manjaro Linux"
ID=manjaro
ID_LIKE=arch
BUILD_ID=rolling
$ uname -a
Linux 6.14.6-2-MANJARO #1 SMP PREEMPT_DYNAMIC Wed, 14 May 2025 14:56:34 +0000 x86_64 GNU/Linux
```

</details>


### Install tools

<details>

</details>

kubernetes/kubernetes#137950.body: hoisted from https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/137946#issuecomment-4103730222 these were the targets in that job that got noticeably worse between 3/13 and 3/19 when PLEGOnDemandRelist was enabled: https://storage.googleapis.com/k8s-triage/index.html?test=Containers%20Lifecycle#325c6ed46a2444ec9edd * `E2eNode Suite [It] [sig-node] [NodeConformance] Containers Lifecycle when A pod with restartable init containers is terminating when The PreStop hooks don't exit should terminate sidecars simultaneously if prestop doesn't exit` * `couldn't find that restartable-init-2 started after restartable-init-1` * https://storage.googleapis.com/k8s-triage/index.html?test=Containers%20Lifecycle#27dc49de6944e0cb8c33 * `E2eNode Suite [It] [sig-node] [NodeConformance] Containers Lifecycle when A pod with restartable init containers is terminating when the restartable init containers have multiple PreStop hooks should call sidecar container PreStop hook simultaneously` * `couldn't find that restartable-init-2 started after restartable-init-1` * https://storage.googleapis.com/k8s-triage/index.html?test=Containers%20Lifecycle#05ab234bcdf71920db30 * `E2eNode Suite [It] [sig-node] [Serial] Containers Lifecycle when The kubelet restarts when a Pod is initialized and running should not restart any completed init container after the kubelet restart` * Timeout * https://storage.googleapis.com/k8s-triage/index.html?test=Containers%20Lifecycle#68509267ef978143664a * `E2eNode Suite [It] [sig-node] [NodeConformance] [Serial] Containers Lifecycle when A node running restartable init containers reboots when A node is rebooting and receives an update request should handle an update during the node reboot` * Timeout * _Originally posted by @liggitt in https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/137946#issuecomment-4103730222_ /sig node /assign @tallclair /milestone v1.36 It is possible the tests were fragile / incorrect and the fix is a test-only improvement, but we need to understand the degraded test health and resolve this before re-enabling PLEGOnDemandRelist

### Cherry picks

<!--
Depending on the Golang release type, this section may not be required.

General rule of thumb:
Only cherry pick Golang patch releases to branches that have the same Golang
minor release version.

Concrete example:
At the time of this template's creation, go1.15.5 was just merged on our
primary development branch and the following Golang versions were active on
in-support kubernetes/kubernetes release branches:
- `master`: go1.15.5
- `release-1.19`: go1.15.2
- `release-1.18`: go1.13.15
- `release-1.17`: go1.13.15

In this case, we would only cherry pick the go1.15.5 to the `release-1.19`
branch, since it is the only other branch with a go1.15 minor version on it.
-->

- [x] Kubernetes 1.34: https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/135612
- [x] Kubernetes 1.33: https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/135613
- [x] Kubernetes 1.32: https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/135614

<!--
  If the Golang version of the active development branch (`master`) is newer than
any of the Golang versions on _active_ release branches, then the current
Golang versions for all release branches need to be updated within publishing
bot rules.
  Concrete example:
  - `master` was just updated from go1.16.6 to go1.16.7
  - cherry picks were issued to the 1.22 and 1.21 branches
  - `release-1.20` was also updated from go1.15.14 to go1.15.15
  - these changes were cherry picked to the 1.19 branch

  In this case, because we updated the default go version on `master` to
go1.16.7, there's no action required for staging repositories using go1.16.
  However, for staging repository branches using go1.15, the `master` branch's
publishing bot rules need to be updated to learn about the Golang update that
happened for the 1.20 and 1.19 Kubernetes release branches.
  PR: https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/122299
-->
- [x] publishing bot rule updates for active Golang versions: https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/135620

images can use the latest available release.

Example: https://github.com/kubernetes/release/pull/3394
-->

  - `master` was just updated from go1.16.6 to go1.16.7
  - cherry picks were issued to the 1.22 and 1.21 branches
  - `release-1.20` was also updated from go1.15.14 to go1.15.15
  - these changes were cherry picked to the 1.19 branch

  In this case, because we updated the default go version on `master` to
go1.16.7, there's no action required for staging repositories using go1.16.
  However, for staging repository branches using go1.15, the `master` branch's
publishing bot rules need to be updated to learn about the Golang update that
happened for the 1.20 and 1.19 Kubernetes release branches.
  PR: https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/122299
-->

kubernetes/test-infra#33827.body:
<!-- Please only use this template for submitting enhancement requests -->

**What would you like to be added**:

https://testgrid.k8s.io/sig-node-cri-o#ci-crio-cgroupv1-node-e2e-unlabelled

These jobs are a grabbag of all incorrectly labeled tests. We should label these tests and eventually drop this test lane. 

**Why is this needed**:

Many of these tests could be considered conformance and having a non standard labeling is a bit confusing.
kubernetes/test-infra#33827.title: Drop unlabeled test lane

kubernetes/website#43993.body:
The page at https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/instrumentation/metrics/ _details the metrics that different Kubernetes components export_. However it never mentions which component actually exports which metric, e.g. the `kube_pod_resource_limit` and `kube_pod_resource_request` metrics are exported by kube-scheduler but `node_cpu_usage_seconds_total` is coming from node-exporter.

It would be great to enhance that page with information about which component exports a particular metric and which metric endpoint of that component does that. Using that information people can write Prometheus jobs that scrape the endpoints of components to get the metrics they are interested in.
kubernetes/website#43993.title: Document metric source
